Suppose every smooth function on $(M,g)$ satisfies $\|u-u_M\|_{s-1}\le
  S\|du\|_1$ for some fixed $s>1$. Then for $1\le p<s$,
  \[
    \|u\|_{\frac{sp}{s-p}}\le\frac{p(s-1)}{s-p}S\|du\|_p+\|u\|_p.
  \]

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-rellich-compactness
entries.title: Theorem 7.1.18 — Rellich Compactness
entries.body:
Assume $(M^n,g)$ is a compact Riemannian $n$-manifold. Then the inclusion
  $W^{1,2}(M)\subset L^2(M)$ (where $W^{1,2}(M)$ is the Hilbert-space closure
  of $C^\infty(M)$ in the norm $\|u\|_2^2+\|du\|_2^2$) is compact.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch7-gromov-generators
entries.title: Lemma 7.2.1 — Gromov, 1980
entries.body:
A compact Riemannian manifold $M$ admits generators $\{c_1,\dots,c_m\}$ for
  $\Gamma=\pi_1(M)$ such that all relations are of the form
  $c_ic_jc_k^{-1}=1$ for suitable $i,j,k$, with each generator represented by a
  loop of length $\le3\diam(M)$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-lens-spaces-example
entries.title: Example 7.2.2
entries.body:
Consider $M_k=S^3/\mathbb{Z}_k$, the constant curvature $3$-sphere quotient by
  the cyclic group of order $k$. As $k\to\infty$, $\vol M_k\to0$ while the
  curvature stays $1$ and $\diam M_k=\pi/2$. Thus $\pi_1(M_k)=\mathbb{Z}_k$ grows
  only at the expense of shrinking volume; writing $\mathbb{Z}_k$ via generators as
  in \cref{lem:pet-ch7-gromov-generators} requires the number of generators to
  go to infinity with $k$, foreshadowing \cref{thm:pet-ch7-anderson-finiteness}.

entries.label: def:pet-ch7-manifold-class
entries.title: the class $\mathfrak{M}(n,k,v,D)$
entries.body:
For $n\in\mathbb{N}$, $k\in\mathbb{R}$, $v,D\in(0,\infty)$, let
  $\mathfrak{M}(n,k,v,D)$ denote the class of compact Riemannian $n$-manifolds
  $(M,g)$ with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$, $\vol M\ge v$, $\diam M\le D$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-anderson-finiteness
entries.title: Theorem 7.2.3 — Anderson, 1990
entries.body:
For fixed $n,k,v,D$, there are only finitely many isomorphism types of
  fundamental group among the manifolds in $\mathfrak{M}(n,k,v,D)$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch7-anderson-short-loops
entries.title: Lemma 7.2.4 — Anderson, 1990
entries.body:
For fixed $n,k,v,D$ there are $L=L(n,k,v,D)$, $N=N(n,k,v,D)$ such that for
  every $M\in\mathfrak{M}(n,k,v,D)$, any subgroup of $\pi_1(M)$ generated by
  loops of length $\le L$ has order $\le N$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-cheng-rigidity
entries.title: Theorem 7.2.5 — S. Y. Cheng, 1975
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is complete with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k>0$ and $\diam=\pi/\sqrt k$, then
  $(M,g)$ is isometric to $S^n_k$ (the space form of curvature $k$).

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-anderson-cp2-example
entries.title: Example 7.2.6 — Anderson, 1990
entries.body:
For $n=4$, consider metrics $dr^2+\rho^2\sigma_1^2+\phi^2(\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2)$
  on $I\times S^3$, with
  \[
    \rho(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\sin(ar)}{a}&r\le r_0,\\c_1\sin(r+\delta)&r\ge
    r_0,\end{cases}\qquad
    \phi(r)=\begin{cases}br^2+c&r\le r_0,\\c_2\sin(r+\delta)&r\ge
    r_0,\end{cases}
  \]
  reflected in $r=\pi/2-\delta$ to produce a metric on $\mathbb{CP}^2\#\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$.
  For any small $r_0>0$ the parameters can be tuned so $\rho,\phi$ are $C^1$
  and generate a metric with $\Ric\ge3$; as $r_0\to0$ the parameter interval
  $I\to[0,\pi]$, so the diameters converge to $\pi$, showing that
  \cref{thm:pet-ch7-cheng-rigidity} does not admit a topological rigidity
  refinement when $n=4$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-otsu-example
entries.title: Example 7.2.7 — Otsu, 1991
entries.body:
For $n\ge5$, standard doubly warped products $dr^2+\rho^2ds_2^2+\phi^2ds^2_{n-3}$
  on $I\times S^2\times S^{n-3}$, with $\rho,\phi$ chosen analogously to
  \cref{ex:pet-ch7-anderson-cp2-example}, yield metrics on $S^2\times
  S^{n-2}$ with $\Ric\ge n-1$ and diameter $\to\pi$. In both examples the
  functions $\rho,\phi$ are only $C^1$ at the outset, but being concave they
  can be smoothed near the break points while remaining concave, changing
  values and first derivatives negligibly and only increasing the (already
  nonpositive) second derivative in absolute value, so the lower Ricci bound
  persists after smoothing.

entries.label: def:pet-ch7-ray-line
entries.title: ray, line
entries.body:
A \emph{ray} $r:[0,\infty)\to(M,g)$ is a unit-speed geodesic with
  $|r(t)r(s)|=|t-s|$ for all $t,s\ge0$; it may be viewed as a semi-infinite
  segment, or a segment from $r(0)$ to infinity. A \emph{line}
  $l:\mathbb{R}\to(M,g)$ is a unit-speed geodesic with $|l(t)l(s)|=|t-s|$ for all
  $t,s\in\mathbb{R}$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch7-connected-infinity
entries.title: connected/disconnected at infinity
entries.body:
A complete manifold $(M,g)$ is \emph{connected at infinity} if for every
  compact $K\subset M$ there is a compact $C\supset K$ such that any two
  points of $M-C$ can be joined by a curve in $M-K$. Otherwise $(M,g)$ is
  \emph{disconnected at infinity}.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch7-ray-line-existence
entries.title: Lemma 7.3.1
entries.body:
If $p\in(M,g)$ then a ray emanating from $p$ always exists. If $M$ is
  disconnected at infinity, $(M,g)$ contains a line.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-paraboloid-no-lines
entries.title: Example 7.3.2

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-cylinder-contains-line
entries.title: Example 7.3.3
entries.body:
Any complete metric on $S^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R}$ contains a line, since the
  manifold is disconnected at infinity.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-schwarzschild-no-line
entries.title: Example 7.3.4
entries.body:
The Schwarzschild metric on $S^{n-2}\times\mathbb{R}^2$ contains no lines, as
  follows from the splitting theorem (\cref{thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem})
  since the space is not metrically a product.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch7-busemann-approximants
entries.title: Proposition 7.3.6

entries.label: def:pet-ch7-busemann-function
entries.title: Busemann function
entries.body:
By \cref{prop:pet-ch7-busemann-approximants}, $b_t$ converges pointwise as
  $t\to\infty$ to a $1$-Lipschitz function
  \[
    b_\infty(x)=\lim_{t\to\infty}b_t(x),\qquad |b_\infty(x)-b_\infty(y)|\le|xy|,
    \qquad |b_\infty(x)|\le|xc(0)|,
  \]
  satisfying $b_\infty(c(r))=\lim_{t\to\infty}(|c(r)c(t)|-t)=-r$. This
  $b_\infty$, denoted $b_c$, is the \emph{Busemann function} for $c$, thought
  of as a renormalized distance function from ``$c(\infty)$.''

entries.label: ex:pet-ch7-euclidean-busemann
entries.title: Example 7.3.7
entries.body:
If $M=(\mathbb{R}^n,g_{\mathbb{R}^n})$, every Busemann function has the form
  $b_c(x)=\dot c(0)\cdot(c(0)-x)$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch7-horosphere-asymptote
entries.title: horosphere, asymptote
entries.body:
The level sets $b_c^{-1}(t)$ are \emph{horospheres} (hyperplanes in $\mathbb{R}^n$;
  spheres tangent to the boundary in the Poincaré model of hyperbolic space).
  Given a ray $c$ and $p\in M$, a family of unit-speed segments
  $\sigma_i:[0,L_i]\to M$ from $p$ to $c(t_i)$, $t_i\to\infty$, subconverges (as
  in \cref{lem:pet-ch7-ray-line-existence}) to a ray $\tilde c:[0,\infty)\to
  M$, $\tilde c(0)=p$, called an \emph{asymptote} for $c$ from $p$; asymptotes
  need not be unique.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch7-busemann-asymptote-relation
entries.title: Proposition 7.3.8

entries.label: lem:pet-ch7-busemann-superharmonic
entries.title: Lemma 7.3.9
entries.body: If $\Ric(M,g)\ge0$, then $\Delta b_c\le0$ everywhere.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem
entries.title: Theorem 7.3.5 — The Splitting Theorem, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ contains a line and has $\Ric\ge0$, then $(M,g)$ is isometric to
  a product $(H\times\mathbb{R},\,g_0+dt^2)$.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.5 — The Splitting Theorem, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem

entries.label: cor:pet-ch7-no-ricci-flat-sphere-product
entries.title: Corollary 7.3.10
entries.body: $S^k\times S^l$ admits no Ricci flat metric when $k=2,3$.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.5 — The Splitting Theorem, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem
entries.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.5 — The Splitting Theorem, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-wilking-remark
entries.title: Remark 7.3.12
entries.body:
Wilking has shown conversely that any group $G$ admitting a finite normal
  subgroup $H\subset G$ with $G/H$ acting discretely and cocompactly on a
  Euclidean space is the fundamental group of a compact manifold with
  nonnegative sectional curvature.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: cor:pet-ch7-flat-manifold-Kpi1
entries.title: Corollary 7.3.13
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is compact with $\Ric\ge0$. If $M$ is a $K(\pi,1)$ (its
  universal cover is contractible), then the universal cover is Euclidean
  space and $(M,g)$ is flat.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: cor:pet-ch7-finite-fundamental-group-positive-ricci
entries.title: Corollary 7.3.14
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is compact with $\Ric\ge0$ and $\Ric>0$ on some tangent space
  $T_pM$, then $\pi_1(M)$ is finite.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: cor:pet-ch7-first-betti-number-bound
entries.title: Corollary 7.3.15
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is compact with $\Ric\ge0$, then $b_1(M)\le\dim M=n$, with
  equality iff $(M,g)$ is a flat torus.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: thm:pet-ch7-homogeneous-splitting
entries.title: Theorem 7.3.16
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a homogeneous Riemannian manifold with $\Ric\ge0$. Then
  $(M,g)=(N\times\mathbb{R}^k,\,g_N+g_{\mathbb{R}^k})$ with $(N,g_N)$ compact homogeneous.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.5 — The Splitting Theorem, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-splitting-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-1
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.1
entries.body:
With notation as in §7.1.1 and using $\vol=\lambda\,dr\wedge\vol_{n-1}$, show
  that $\mu=\lambda^{1/(n-1)}$ satisfies
  \[
    \partial_r^2\mu\le-\frac{\mu}{n-1}\Ric(\partial_r,\partial_r),\qquad
    \mu(0,\theta)=0,\qquad \lim_{r\to0}\partial_r\mu(r,\theta)=1,
  \]
  and that this yields the desired volume-form estimates.

deps.title: polar density and its constant-curvature model
deps.label: def:pet-ch7-polar-density

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-2
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.2 — Calabi and Yau
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete noncompact with $\Ric\ge0$, $p\in M$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show that for each $R>1$ there is $x\in M$ with
    \[
      \vol B(p,1)\le\vol B(x,R+1)-\vol B(x,R-1)
      \le\frac{(R+1)^n-(R-1)^n}{(R+1)^n}\vol B(p,2R).
    \]
    \item[(2)] Show there is $C>0$ with $\vol B(p,R)\ge CR$.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-3
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.3
entries.body:
Let $f:I\to\mathbb{R}$ be continuous on an interval $I\subset\mathbb{R}$. Show that (1)
  $f$ is convex, (2) $f$ has a linear support function from below of the form
  $a(x-x_0)+f(x_0)$ at every $x_0\in I$, and (3) $f''\ge0$ in the support
  sense at every $x_0\in I$, are equivalent.

deps.title: support functions; barrier-sense Hessian and Laplacian bounds
deps.label: def:pet-ch7-support-function

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-4
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.4
entries.body:
Show that on a compact Riemannian manifold there is no finite $S(s)$ with
  $\|f-f_M\|_\infty\le S\|df\|_1$ for $1<s<\dim M$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-5
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.5 — Basic Covering Lemma
entries.body:
Given a separable metric space $(X,d)$ and a bounded positive function
  $R:X\to(0,D]$, show there is a countable $A\subset X$ such that the balls
  $B(p,R(p))$, $p\in A$, are pairwise disjoint and $X=\bigcup_{p\in
  A}B(p,5R(p))$. (Hint: choose the points successively so that
  $R(p_{k+1})\ge\tfrac12\sup_{p\in X\setminus\bigcup_iB(p_i,2R(p_i))}R(p)$.)

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-6
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.6
entries.body:
Assume $r(x)=|xp|$ is smooth on $B(p,R)$. Show that if
  $\Hess r=(\snk'(r)/\snk(r))g_r$ in polar coordinates, then all sectional
  curvatures on $B(p,R)$ equal $k$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-7
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.7
entries.body:
Construct convex surfaces in $\mathbb{R}^3$ by capping off cylinders
  $[-R,R]\times S^1$ to show the Sobolev--Poincaré constants increase as $R$
  increases. (Hint: consider test functions constant except on $[-1,1]\times
  S^1$.)

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-8
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.8
entries.body:
Show that if $(M,g)$ has $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$ and $\vol B(p,R)=v(n,k,R)$ for some
  $p\in M$, then the metric has constant curvature $k$ on $B(p,R)$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-9
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.9
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a vector field and $F_t(p)=\exp_p(tX|_p)$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] For $v\in T_pM$, show $J(t)=DF_t(v)$ is a Jacobi field along
      $c(t)=\exp(tX)$ with $J(0)=v$, $\dot J(0)=\nabla_vX$.
    \item[(2)] For an orthonormal basis $e_i$ of $T_pM$ and $J_i(t)=DF_t(e_i)$,
      show $(\det[DF_t])^2=\det[g(J_i(t),J_j(t))]$.
    \item[(3)] Show that as long as $\det(DF_t)\ne0$,
      \[
        \frac{d^2(\det(DF_t))^{1/n}}{dt^2}
        \le-\frac{(\det(DF_t))^{1/n}}{n}\Ric(\dot c,\dot c).
      \]
      (Hint: use $(\operatorname{tr}A)^2\le n\operatorname{tr}(A^*A)$ for
      $n\times n$ matrices $A$.)
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-10
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.10
entries.body:
Show that a complete manifold $(M,g)$ with $\Ric\ge0$ and
  $\lim_{r\to\infty}\vol B(p,r)/(\omega_nr^n)=1$ for some $p\in M$ must be
  isometric to Euclidean space.

deps.title: Lemma 7.1.4 — Relative Volume Comparison, Bishop, 1964, and Gromov, 1980
deps.label: lem:pet-ch7-relative-volume-comparison

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-11
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.11
entries.body:
Show that any function on an $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifold satisfies
  $|\Hess u|^2\ge\tfrac1n|\Delta u|^2$, with equality iff
  $\Hess u=\tfrac{\Delta u}{n}g$. What can be said about $M$ when
  $\Hess u=\tfrac{\Delta u}{n}g$?

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-12
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.12
entries.body:
Show that if $u,v:M\to\mathbb{R}$ are compactly supported and smooth on open dense
  sets, then
  \[
    \int u\,\Delta v\,\vol=\int v\,\Delta u\,\vol
    =-\int g(du,dv)\,\vol=-\int g(\nabla u,\nabla v)\,\vol.
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-13
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.13
entries.body:
Show that if $\Delta u=\lambda u$ on a closed Riemannian manifold, then
  $\lambda\le0$, and $\lambda=0$ forces $u$ constant.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-14
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.14
entries.body:
Show that $u_k=\cos(\sqrt kr)$ on $S^n_k=S^n(1/\sqrt k)$ satisfies $\Delta
  u_k=-nk\,u_k$ and $\int u_k\,\vol=0$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-15
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.15 — Lichnerowicz
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)$ be closed with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k>0$. Use the Bochner formula to
  show that every $u$ with $\Delta u=-\lambda u$, $\lambda>0$, satisfies
  $\lambda\ge nk$. Deduce, via the spectral theorem for $\Delta$, that every
  $u$ with $\int u\,\vol=0$ satisfies the Poincaré inequality
  $\int u^2\,\vol\le\tfrac1{nk}\int|du|^2\,\vol$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-16
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.16 — Obata
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)$ be closed with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k>0$. Using the Bochner formula as
  in \cref{rem:pet-ch7-ex-15}, show that if $\Delta u=-nk\,u$ for some
  function $u$, then $\Hess u=-ku\,g$. Conclude $(M^n,g)=S^n_k$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-17
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.17 — P. Li and Schoen
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete with $\Ric\ge-(n-1)k^2$, $k\ge0$; $p\in M$; $R>0$
  with $\partial B(p,2R)\ne\emptyset$; $q\in\partial B(p,2R)$;
  $r(x)=|xq|$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show $\Delta r\le(n-1)(R^{-1}+k)$ on $B(p,R)$.
    \item[(2)] Let $f(x)=a\exp(-ar(x))$, $a>0$. Show
      $\Delta f\ge a\exp(-3aR)\big(a-(n-1)(R^{-1}+k)\big)$.
    \item[(3)] Let $u\ge0$ be smooth with compact support in $B(p,R)$, and
      choose $a=n(R^{-1}+k)$. Using
      $\int_{B(p,R)}u\,\Delta f\,\vol=-\int_{B(p,R)}g(du,df)\,\vol$, show
      $\int_{B(p,R)}u\,\vol\le C\int_{B(p,R)}|du|\,\vol$, where
      $C=\dfrac{R}{1+kR}\exp\big(2n(1+kR)\big)$.
    \item[(4)] Extend the inequality of (3) to all smooth $u$ with compact
      support in $B(p,R)$.
    \item[(5)] For $s\ge1$ and $u$ with compact support in $B(p,R)$, show
      $\int_{B(p,R)}|u|^s\,\vol\le(sC)^s\int_{B(p,R)}|du|\,\vol$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-18
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.18 — Cheeger
entries.body:
Suppose $(M^n,g)$ has $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] For $p_1,\dots,p_k\in M$, show
      $r\mapsto\vol\big(\bigcup_{i=1}^kB(p_i,r)\big)/v(n,k,r)$ is
      nonincreasing and converges to $k$ as $r\to0$.
    \item[(2)] For $A\subset M$, show
      $r\mapsto\vol\big(\bigcup_{p\in A}B(p,r)\big)/v(n,k,r)$ is
      nonincreasing, by applying (1) to a dense finite subset of $A$.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Lemma 7.1.4 — Relative Volume Comparison, Bishop, 1964, and Gromov, 1980
deps.label: lem:pet-ch7-relative-volume-comparison

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-19
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.19
entries.body:
For $p\in M$ and $\Gamma\subset T_pM$ a set of unit vectors, define the
  polar cone $B^\Gamma(p,R)=\{(t,\theta)\in M\mid t\le R,\ \theta\in\Gamma\}$.
  If $\Ric M\ge(n-1)k$, show
  \[
    \vol B^\Gamma(p,R)\le\vol\Gamma\cdot\int_0^R(\snk_k(t))^{n-1}\,dt.
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-20
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.20
entries.body:
Let $G$ be a compact connected Lie group with a biinvariant metric and
  $\Ric\ge0$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] If $G$ has finite center, show $\pi_1(G)$ is finite.
    \item[(2)] Show a finite cover of $G$ has the form $G'\times T^k$ with
      $G'$ compact simply connected and $T^k$ a torus.
    \item[(3)] If $\pi_1(G)$ is finite, show the center of $G$ is finite.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-21
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.21
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be $n$-dimensional and isometric to Euclidean space outside a
  compact $K\subset M$, i.e.\ $M-K$ is isometric to $\mathbb{R}^n-C$ for some
  compact $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$. If $\Ric\ge0$, show $M=\mathbb{R}^n$.

deps.title: Theorem 7.3.11 — Structure Theorem for Nonnegative Ricci Curvature, Cheeger and Gromoll, 1971
deps.label: thm:pet-ch7-structure-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch7-ex-22
entries.title: Exercise 7.5.22
entries.body:
Show that if $\Ric\ge n-1$ then $\diam\le\pi$, by showing that if
  $|pq|>\pi$, then $e_{p,q}(x)=|px|+|xq|-|pq|$ has negative Laplacian at a
  local minimum.

entries.label: def:pet-ch8-killing-field
entries.title: Killing field
entries.body:
A vector field $X$ on a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is called a \emph{Killing
  field} if the local flows generated by $X$ act by isometries.

lean.name: PetersenLib.IsKillingField
lean.code:
def IsKillingField (g : RiemannianMetric I M) (X : Π x : M, TangentSpace I x) : Prop :=
  ∀ (Y : Fin 2 → Π x : M, TangentSpace I x), (∀ i, IsSmoothVectorField (Y i)) →
    lieDerivativeTensor I X (metricOperator g) Y = 0

/-! ## The Hessian via the Lie derivative -/

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-killing-lie-derivative
entries.title: Proposition 8.1.1
entries.body:
A vector field $X$ on a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is a Killing field if and
  only if $L_Xg=0$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-killing-skew-symmetric
entries.title: Proposition 8.1.2
entries.body:
A vector field $X$ is a Killing field if and only if $v\mapsto
abla_vX$ is a
  skew-symmetric $(1,1)$-tensor, i.e.\ $g(
abla_vX,w)=-g(
abla_wX,v)$ for all
  $v,w$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-killing-curvature-identities
entries.title: Proposition 8.1.3
entries.body:
If $X\in\iso(M,g)$, then
  \[
    \nabla^2_{V,W}X=-R(X,V)W.
  \]
  If $X,Y\in\iso(M,g)$, then
  \[
    [\nabla X,\nabla Y](V)+\nabla_V[X,Y]=R(X,Y)V.
  \]

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-killing-uniqueness
entries.title: Proposition 8.1.4
entries.body:
For a given $p\in M$, a Killing field $X\in\iso(M,g)$ is uniquely determined by
  $X|_p$ and $(\nabla X)|_p$. In particular there is a short exact sequence
  \[
    0\to\iso_p\to\iso(M,g)\to t_p\to0,
  \]
  where $\iso_p=\{X\in\iso(M,g)\mid X|_p=0\}$ and
  $t_p=\{X|_p\in T_pM\mid X\in\iso(M,g)\}$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-zero-set-totally-geodesic
entries.title: Theorem 8.1.5
entries.body:
The zero set of a Killing field is a disjoint union of totally geodesic
  submanifolds, each of even codimension.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-isometry-lie-algebra-dimension
entries.title: Theorem 8.1.6
entries.body:
The set of Killing fields $\iso(M,g)$ is a Lie algebra of dimension
  $\le\binom{n+1}{2}$. Furthermore, if $M$ is complete, then $\iso(M,g)$ is the
  Lie algebra of $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$.

deps.title: Lie group structure of the isometry group and the one-parameter-subgroup correspondence
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-killing-flow-oneparameter-subgroup

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-killing-flow-oneparameter-subgroup
entries.title: Lie group structure of the isometry group and the one-parameter-subgroup correspondence
entries.body:
Endow $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ with the compact-open topology, so convergence means
  uniform convergence on compact sets. If $M$ is complete, this topology makes
  $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ into a Lie group whose Lie algebra is $\iso(M,g)$: the
  flows of Killing fields are then defined for all time and consist of
  isometries, yielding differentiable one-parameter subgroups of
  $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$, and conversely every differentiable one-parameter
  subgroup of $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ arises as the flow of a Killing field; this
  correspondence identifies the Lie algebra of $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ with
  $\iso(M,g)$.

deps.title: Lie group structure of the isometry group and the one-parameter-subgroup correspondence
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-killing-flow-oneparameter-subgroup

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-max-isometry-space-forms
entries.title: maximal-dimensional isometry groups among space-form quotients
entries.body:
For the simply connected space forms $S^n_0$ (of constant curvature $0$),
  $\dim(\mathrm{Iso}(S^n_0))=\binom{n+1}2$, the maximal possible value. Among
  other complete constant-curvature manifolds $S^n_0/\Gamma$ with
  $\Gamma\subset\mathrm{Iso}(S^n_0)$ acting freely and discontinuously, the
  isometry group has maximal dimension $\binom{n+1}2$ only for
  $\Gamma=\{\pm I\}$ (the sphere itself is $\Gamma=\{I\}$), i.e.\ only for
  $\mathbb{RP}^n$ in addition to $S^n_0$ itself.

deps.title: maximal-dimensional isometry groups among space-form quotients
deps.label: rem:pet-ch8-max-isometry-space-forms

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-maximal-symmetry-constant-curvature
entries.title: maximal symmetry implies constant curvature
entries.body: If $\dim(\iso(M,g))=\binom{n+1}2$, then $(M,g)$ has constant curvature.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-killing-norm-formulas
entries.title: Proposition 8.2.1
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a Killing field on $(M,g)$ and $f=\tfrac12g(X,X)=\tfrac12|X|^2$.
  Then
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item $\nabla f=-\nabla_XX$.
    \item $\mathrm{Hess}\,f(V,V)=|\nabla_VX|^2-R(V,X,X,V)$.
    \item $\Delta f=|\nabla X|^2-\mathrm{Ric}(X,X)$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-bochner-negative-ricci
entries.title: Theorem 8.2.2 (Bochner, 1946)
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is compact with $\mathrm{Ric}\le0$. Then every Killing field
  is parallel. Furthermore, if $\mathrm{Ric}<0$, then there are no nontrivial
  Killing fields.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch8-isometry-dimension-bound-negative-ricci
entries.title: Corollary 8.2.3
entries.body:
With $(M,g)$ as in \cref{thm:pet-ch8-bochner-negative-ricci},
  \[
    \dim(\iso(M,g))=\dim(\mathrm{Iso}(M,g))\le\dim M,
  \]
  and $\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ is finite if $\mathrm{Ric}(M,g)<0$.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch8-splitting-negative-ricci
entries.title: Corollary 8.2.4
entries.body:
With $(M,g)$ as in \cref{thm:pet-ch8-bochner-negative-ricci} and
  $k=\dim(\iso(M,g))$, the universal cover splits isometrically as
  $\widetilde M=\mathbb{R}^k\times N$.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch8-homogeneous-negative-ricci-flat
entries.title: Corollary 8.2.5
entries.body:
A compact homogeneous space with $\mathrm{Ric}\le0$ is flat. In particular,
  any Ricci-flat homogeneous space is flat.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-quasi-negative-ricci-no-killing
entries.title: Theorem 8.2.6
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is a compact manifold with \emph{quasi-negative} Ricci
  curvature, i.e.\ $\mathrm{Ric}\le0$ everywhere and $\mathrm{Ric}(v,v)<0$ for
  all $v\in T_pM\setminus\{0\}$ for some $p\in M$. Then $(M,g)$ admits no
  nontrivial Killing field.

entries.label: def:pet-ch8-f-structure
entries.title: $F$-structure
entries.body:
An \emph{$F$-structure} on $M$ consists of a finite covering of open sets
  $U_i$ of some finite covering space $\widetilde M\to M$, together with a
  Killing field $X_i$ on each $U_i$, such that whenever $U_i\cap U_j\ne\varnothing$
  the fields commute there: $[X_i,X_j]=0$ on $U_i\cap U_j$. The $F$-structure is
  \emph{pure} of positive rank when, roughly, the collection of local Killing
  fields is nowhere all simultaneously trivial in a suitable sense, measured via
  the function $f=\det[g(X_i,X_j)]$ (which reduces to $f=g(X,X)$ when only one
  field is given globally).

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-rong-f-structure
entries.title: Rong's generalization of Bochner's theorem
entries.body:
X. Rong generalized \cref{thm:pet-ch8-bochner-negative-ricci} to show that a
  closed Riemannian manifold with negative Ricci curvature admits no pure
  $F$-structure of positive rank. The strategy mirrors the proof of
  \cref{thm:pet-ch8-bochner-negative-ricci}: one shows the function
  $f=\det[g(X_i,X_j)]$ from \cref{def:pet-ch8-f-structure} is a well-defined,
  sufficiently regular function on $M$ admitting a Bochner-type formula.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-hopf-conjecture-low-dimensions
entries.title: the Hopf conjecture and low-dimensional cases
entries.body:
Every vector field on an even-dimensional sphere has a zero, since
  $\chi(S^{2m})=2\ne0$. H. Hopf conjectured that every even-dimensional compact
  manifold with positive sectional curvature has positive Euler characteristic.
  If the curvature operator is positive, this holds because then
  $\chi(M)=2$; if the Ricci curvature is positive, the fundamental group is
  finite, so $H_1(M,\mathbb{R})=0$, which already establishes the conjecture in
  dimension $2$. In dimension $4$, Poincar\'e duality with $H_1(M,\mathbb{R})=H_3(M,\mathbb{R})=0$
  gives $\chi(M)=1+\dim H_2(M,\mathbb{R})+1\ge2$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-berger-killing-field-zero
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.1 (Berger, 1965)
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is a compact, even-dimensional manifold of positive sectional
  curvature, then every Killing field has a zero.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-berger-odd-dimension-counterexample
entries.title: failure in odd dimensions
entries.body:
\cref{thm:pet-ch8-berger-killing-field-zero} fails in odd dimensions: the
  unit vector field generating the Hopf fibration $S^3(1)\to S^2(1/2)$ is a
  Killing field on the positively curved, odd-dimensional manifold $S^3(1)$
  that is nowhere zero.

entries.label: def:pet-ch8-euler-characteristic
entries.title: Euler characteristic
entries.body:
The \emph{Euler characteristic} of a space $M$ is the alternating sum
  \[
    \chi(M)=\sum_{p=0}^n(-1)^p\dim H_p(M,\mathbb{R}).
  \]

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-euler-characteristic-zero-set-sum
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.2
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a Killing field on a compact Riemannian manifold $M$, and let
  $N_i\subset M$ be the components of the zero set of $X$. Then
  $\chi(M)=\sum_i\chi(N_i)$.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch8-positive-euler-characteristic-6manifold
entries.title: Corollary 8.3.3
entries.body:
If $M$ is a compact $6$-manifold with positive sectional curvature that
  admits a Killing field, then $\chi(M)>0$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-isolated-zero-orbit-angle-bound
entries.title: orbit angle-sum bound for an isolated zero
entries.body:
Let $p$ be an isolated zero of a Killing field $X$ on a Riemannian
  $4$-manifold, with flow $F^t$ inducing an isometric, fixed-point-free action
  $R^t=DF^t|_p$ on $S^3\subset T_pM$. Decomposing $T_pM=V_1\oplus V_2$ into the
  orthogonal invariant subspaces for this action exhibits $S^3$ as a doubly
  warped product over $[0,\pi/2]$, with associated distance function $r$
  measuring the angle to $V_1$; for all $v_1,v_2,v_3\in S^3$,
  \[
    r(v_1)r(v_2)+r(v_1)r(v_3)+r(v_2)r(v_3)\le\pi,
  \]
  and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exist $t_1,t_2,t_3$ such that
  \[
    \angle(R^{t_1}v_1,R^{t_2}v_2)+\angle(R^{t_1}v_1,R^{t_3}v_3)+\angle(R^{t_2}v_2,R^{t_3}v_3)\le\pi+\varepsilon.
  \]

deps.title: orbit angle-sum bound for an isolated zero
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-isolated-zero-orbit-angle-bound

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-toponogov-angle-lower-bound
entries.title: Toponogov angle-sum lower bound
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a compact Riemannian $4$-manifold with $\sec\ge1$, and let
  $p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\in M$ be four distinct points such that segments between any
  two of them exist. Writing $\inf\angle p\,x\,q$ for the infimum, over unit
  vectors in the directions of segments from $x$ to $p$ and from $x$ to $q$, of
  the angle between them, Toponogov's comparison theorem (comparing each
  triangle with $p_ip_jp_k$'s sides to a triangle in $S^2(1)$) gives, for each
  choice of three of the four points,
  \[
    \inf\angle p_ip_jp_k+\inf\angle p_kp_ip_j+\inf\angle p_jp_kp_i>\pi.
  \]

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-hsiang-kleiner-4manifold
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.4 (Hsiang and Kleiner, 1989)
entries.body:
If $M^4$ is a compact, orientable, positively curved $4$-manifold that admits
  a Killing field, then $\chi(M)\le3$. In particular, $M$ is topologically
  equivalent to $S^4$ or $\mathbb{CP}^2$.

deps.title: orbit angle-sum bound for an isolated zero
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-isolated-zero-orbit-angle-bound

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-grove-searle-commuting-dependent
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.5 (Grove and Searle, 1994)
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a compact $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature. Two
  commuting Killing fields must be linearly dependent somewhere on $M$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-jacobi-type-field
entries.title: orthogonal fields along a geodesic with drift towards the action
entries.body:
Let $M^n$ have positive sectional curvature, $X$ a Killing field, and let
  $N^{n-k}$ be a component of the zero set of $X$. Let $c:[0,1]\to M$ be a unit
  speed geodesic perpendicular to $N$ at both endpoints. Consider vector fields
  $E$ along $c$, orthogonal to both $c$ and the flow direction of $X$, defined
  by the linear ODE
  \[
    E(0)\in T_{c(0)}N,\qquad
    \dot E=-\frac{g(E,\nabla_{\dot c}X)}{|X|^2}X.
  \]
  Then $g(E,X)\equiv0$; $g(E(1),\nabla_{\dot c(1)}X)=0$; $g(E,\dot c)\equiv0$;
  and the space of such fields $E$ for which $E(1)\in T_{c(1)}N$ has dimension
  at least $n-2k+2$.

deps.title: orthogonal fields along a geodesic with drift towards the action
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-jacobi-type-field

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-wilking-connectedness-principle
entries.title: Lemma 8.3.6 (connectedness principle with symmetries, Wilking, 2003)
entries.body:
Let $M^n$ be a compact $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and $X$
  a Killing field. If $N^{n-k}$ is a component of the zero set of $X$, then
  $N\subset M$ is $(n-2k+2)$-connected.

deps.title: orthogonal fields along a geodesic with drift towards the action
deps.label: lem:pet-ch8-jacobi-type-field

entries.label: lem:pet-ch8-grove-searle-codim2
entries.title: Lemma 8.3.7 (Grove and Searle, 1994)
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a closed $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature. If $M$
  admits a Killing field whose zero set has a component $N$ of codimension
  $2$, then $M$ is diffeomorphic to $S^n$, $\mathbb{CP}^{n/2}$, or a cyclic
  quotient of a sphere $S^n/\mathbb{Z}_q$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch8-commuting-killing-fields-properties
entries.title: Proposition 8.3.8
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be compact and $X,Y\in\iso(M,g)$ commute.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item $Y$ is tangent to the level sets of $|X|^2$, in particular to the
      zero set of $X$.
    \item If $X$ and $Y$ both vanish on a totally geodesic submanifold
      $N\subset M$, then some linear combination of $X,Y$ vanishes on a
      strictly larger connected submanifold.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: def:pet-ch8-symmetry-rank
entries.title: symmetry rank
entries.body:
The \emph{symmetry rank} of a Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is the dimension of
  a maximal subspace (equivalently, Abelian subalgebra) of commuting Killing
  fields in $\iso(M,g)$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-symmetry-rank-half-classification
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.9 (Grove and Searle, 1994)
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a compact $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and
  symmetry rank $k$. If $k\ge n/2$, then $M$ is diffeomorphic to a sphere, a
  complex projective space, or a cyclic quotient of a sphere $S^n/\mathbb{Z}_q$,
  with $\mathbb{Z}_q$ acting by isometries on the unit sphere.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-puttmann-searle-rong
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.10 (Puttmann and Searle, 2002 and Rong, 2002)
entries.body:
If $M^{2n}$ is a compact $2n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and
  symmetry rank $k\ge n/2-1$, then $\chi(M)>0$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-f4-spin8-exception
entries.title: a positively curved exception with lower symmetry rank
entries.body:
\cref{thm:pet-ch8-puttmann-searle-rong} does not cover all known examples:
  the homogeneous space $F_4/\mathrm{Spin}(8)$ is a positively curved
  $24$-manifold with symmetry rank $4$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-wilking-symmetry-rank-classification
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.11 (Wilking, 2003)
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a compact, simply connected, positively curved $n$-manifold with
  symmetry rank $k$, and $n\ge10$. If $k\ge n/4+1$, then $M$ has the topology
  of a sphere, complex projective space, or quaternionic projective space.
  Moreover, when $M$ is not simply connected, its fundamental group is cyclic.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-kennard-2013-euler-characteristic
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.12 (Kennard, 2013)
entries.body:
If $M^{4n}$ is a compact $4n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and
  symmetry rank $k>2\log_4(4n)-2$, then $\chi(M)>0$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch8-kennard-amann-betti-numbers
entries.title: Theorem 8.3.13 (Kennard, 2012 and Amann--Kennard, 2014)
entries.body:
Let $M^{2n}$ be a compact positively curved manifold with symmetry rank
  $k\ge\log_{4/3}(2n)$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item The Betti numbers satisfy $b_2\le1$ and $b_4\le1$.
    \item If $b_4=0$, then $\chi(M)=2$.
    \item Consequently, $M$ cannot have the homotopy type of a product
      $N\times N$ for compact simply connected $N$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-1
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.1
entries.body:
Show that \cref{thm:pet-ch8-isometry-lie-algebra-dimension} does not
  necessarily extend to incomplete Riemannian manifolds.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-2
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.2
entries.body:
Let $N$ be a component of the zero set of a Killing field $X$. Show that
  $\nabla_V(\nabla X)=0$ for vector fields $V$ tangent to $N$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-3
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.3
entries.body:
Show that a coordinate vector field $\partial_i$ is a Killing field if and
  only if $\partial_kg_{ij}=0$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-4
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.4
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a Killing field on $(M,g)$ and $N\subset M$ a submanifold with the
  induced metric.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that if $X$ is tangent to $N$, then $X|_N$ is a Killing field
      on $N$.
    \item Show that if $N$ is totally geodesic, then $X^\top$, the projection
      of $X$ onto $N$, is a Killing field.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-5
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.5
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete Riemannian manifold and $X$ a Killing field on $M$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Let $c:[a,b]\to M$ be a geodesic. Show that there is $\varepsilon>0$
      and a geodesic variation $\bar c:(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\times[a,b]\to M$
      such that the curves $s\mapsto\bar c(s,t)$ are integral curves of $X$.
    \item Use completeness to extend $\bar c$ to
      $(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\times\mathbb{R}\to M$, and show that for all
      $t\in\mathbb{R}$ the curves $s\mapsto\bar c(s,t)$ are integral curves of
      $X$.
    \item Show that the integral curve of $X$ through any point of $M$ exists
      on $(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)$.
    \item Show that $X$ is complete.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-6
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.6
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)$ be a compact Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\dim(\iso(M,g))=n$
  and $\mathrm{Ric}\le0$. Show that $M$ is flat and $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}^n$. (Hint:
  show $\widetilde M=\mathbb{R}^n$ and that the deck transformations have
  differential $I$, since they preserve a parallel orthonormal frame.)

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-7
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.7
entries.body:
Let $x^i$ be standard Cartesian coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^n$, and let
  $W=\mathrm{span}_{\mathbb{R}}\{1,x^1,\dots,x^n\}$,
  $V=\mathrm{span}_{\mathbb{R}}\{x^1,\dots,x^n\}$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that $\iso(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is naturally isomorphic to $\Lambda^2W$,
      identifying $u\wedge v$ with the vector field $u\nabla v-v\nabla u$.
    \item Show similarly that $\iso(S^{n-1}(R))$ is naturally isomorphic to
      $\Lambda^2V$.
    \item Using $\nabla u=g^{ij}\partial_iu\,\partial_j$ for a
      pseudo-Riemannian space, redo (1) for $\mathbb{R}^{p,q}$ and (2) for
      $H^{n-1}(R)\subset\mathbb{R}^{n,1}$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-8
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.8
entries.body:
Let $x^i$ be standard Cartesian coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ and
  $V=\mathrm{span}_{\mathbb{R}}\{1,x^1,\dots,x^{n+1}\}$, restricted to $S^n$.
  Show that for $u,v\in V$ the fields $u\nabla v-v\nabla u$ are conformal (a
  field $X$ is conformal if $L_Xg=\lambda g$ for some function $\lambda$).

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-9
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.9
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $t_p=\{X|_p\in T_pM\mid X\in\iso\}$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that $t_p$ defines an integrable distribution on an open set
      $O\subset M$. (Hint: show
      $\{p\in M\mid\dim t_p=\max_{q\in M}\dim t_q\}$ is open.)
    \item Give an example where $O\ne M$.
    \item Show that $DF(t_p)=t_{F(p)}$ for all $F\in\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$.
    \item Assume $(M,g)$ is complete. Show that the leaves of this
      distribution are homogeneous and properly embedded. (Hint: show the
      connected subgroup $\mathrm{Iso}_0\subset\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$ containing
      the identity is closed and preserves the leaves.)
    \item Show that if $(M,g)$ is homogeneous, then $t_p=T_pM$ for all
      $p\in M$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-10
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.10
entries.body:
Let $\mathfrak t\subset\iso(M,g)$ be an Abelian subalgebra corresponding to a
  torus subgroup $T^k\subset\mathrm{Iso}(M,g)$. Define $\mathfrak p\subset\mathfrak t$
  as the Killing fields corresponding to circle actions, i.e.\ actions induced
  by homomorphisms $S^1\to T^k$. Show that $\mathfrak p$ is a vector space over
  $\mathbb{Q}$ with $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\mathfrak p=\dim_{\mathbb{R}}\mathfrak t$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-11
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.11
entries.body:
Given two Killing fields $X,Y$ on a Riemannian manifold, develop a formula
  for $\Delta g(X,Y)$. Use this to give a formula for the Ricci curvature in a
  frame consisting of Killing fields.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-12
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.12
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a vector field on a Riemannian manifold.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that
      $|L_Xg|^2=2|\nabla X|^2+2\operatorname{tr}(\nabla X\circ\nabla X)$.
    \item Establish, on a closed oriented Riemannian manifold,
      \[
        \int_M\Big(\mathrm{Ric}(X,X)+\operatorname{tr}(\nabla X\circ\nabla X)-(\operatorname{div}X)^2\Big)=0,
      \]
      \[
        \int_M\Big(\mathrm{Ric}(X,X)+g(\operatorname{tr}\nabla^2X,X)+\tfrac12|L_Xg|^2-(\operatorname{div}X)^2\Big)=0.
      \]
    \item Show that $X$ is a Killing field if and only if
      $\operatorname{div}X=0$ and $\operatorname{tr}\nabla^2X=-\mathrm{Ric}(X)$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-13
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.13 (Yano)
entries.body:
If $X$ is an affine vector field, show that
  $\operatorname{tr}\nabla^2X=-\mathrm{Ric}(X)$ and that $\operatorname{div}X$
  is constant. Use this together with the characterizations of
  \cref{rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-12} to show that on closed manifolds affine fields
  are Killing fields.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-14
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.14
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a vector field on a Riemannian manifold. Show that $X$ is a
  Killing field if and only if $L_X$ and $\Delta$ commute on functions.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-15
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.15
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a compact $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and
  $\mathfrak a\subset\iso$ an Abelian subalgebra.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that $\dim\mathfrak a\le n/2$.
    \item Show that spheres and complex projective spaces have maximal
      symmetry rank.
    \item Show that the flat torus $T^n$ has symmetry rank $n$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-16
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.16
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a compact $n$-manifold with positive sectional curvature and
  $\mathfrak a\subset\iso$ an Abelian subalgebra. Let $\mathcal Z(\mathfrak a)$
  be the set of nontrivial connected components of zero sets of Killing fields
  in $\mathfrak a$. Show that if $N\in\mathcal Z(\mathfrak a)$ is maximal under
  inclusion, then $\dim(\mathfrak a|_N)=\dim\mathfrak a-1$ and
  $\dim N\ge2(\dim\mathfrak a-1)$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-17
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.17 (Kennard)
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,\rho)$ be a simply connected compact $n$-manifold with positive
  sectional curvature and symmetry rank $\ge3n/8+1$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item When $n\le8$, conclude that the assumptions are covered by
      \cref{thm:pet-ch8-symmetry-rank-half-classification}.
    \item Use \cref{rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-16} to show that if
      $N\in\mathcal Z(\mathfrak a)$ is maximal under inclusion, then
      $\dim N\ge3n/4$.
    \item Show that a maximal $N\in\mathcal Z(\mathfrak a)$ either has
      codimension $2$ or has symmetry rank $\ge\tfrac{3\dim N}8+1$.
    \item Use induction on $n$ to show that $M$ has the homology groups of a
      sphere or complex projective space. (Hint: when the maximal
      $N\in\mathcal Z(\mathfrak a)$ has codimension $\ge4$, use
      \cref{lem:pet-ch8-wilking-connectedness-principle} to show $N$ is simply
      connected, then use it again to compute homology/homotopy groups in
      dimensions $\le n/2$; finally use Poincar\'e duality to find all
      homology groups of $M$.)
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch8-ex-8-4-18
entries.title: Exercise 8.4.18
entries.body:
Let $\widetilde M\to M$ be the universal covering, with deck transformations
  $\Gamma=\pi_1(M)$ acting as isometries on $\widetilde M$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Show that
      $\iso(M)=\{X\in\iso(\widetilde M)\mid G_*X=X\text{ for all }G\in\Gamma\}$.
    \item Show that the connected Lie subgroup corresponding to
      $\iso(M)\subset\iso(\widetilde M)$ is the identity component of the
      centralizer
      $\mathbf C(\Gamma)=\{F\in\mathrm{Iso}(\widetilde M)\mid FG=GF\text{ for all }G\in\Gamma\}$.
    \item Show that $\mathrm{Iso}(M)$ can be identified with
      $\mathbf N(\Gamma)/\Gamma$, where
      $\mathbf N(\Gamma)=\{F\in\mathrm{Iso}(\widetilde M)\mid F\Gamma F^{-1}=\Gamma\}$
      is the normalizer.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-de-rham-cohomology
entries.title: de Rham complex and cohomology
entries.body:
On a manifold $M$ the \emph{de Rham complex} is
  \[
    0\to\Omega^0(M)\xrightarrow{d^0}\Omega^1(M)\xrightarrow{d^1}\Omega^2(M)\to\cdots
    \xrightarrow{d^{n-1}}\Omega^n(M)\to0,
  \]
  where $\Omega^k(M)$ is the space of $k$-forms on $M$ and $d^k:\Omega^k(M)\to
  \Omega^{k+1}(M)$ is exterior differentiation. The \emph{de Rham cohomology
  groups} $H^k(M)=\ker(d^k)/\operatorname{im}(d^{k-1})$ compute the real
  cohomology of $M$; $H^0(M)\cong\mathbb{R}$ if $M$ is connected, and $H^n(M)\cong\mathbb{R}$
  if $M$ is orientable and compact.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-poincare-duality
entries.title: Poincaré duality pairing
entries.body:
When $M$ is compact and oriented the wedge-and-integrate pairing
  $\Omega^k(M)\times\Omega^{n-k}(M)\to\mathbb{R}$, $(\omega_1,\omega_2)\mapsto
  \int_M\omega_1\wedge\omega_2$, induces a nondegenerate pairing on cohomology
  $H^k(M)\times H^{n-k}(M)\to\mathbb{R}$. Hence $H^k(M)$ and $H^{n-k}(M)$ are dual to
  each other and in particular have the same dimension.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-codifferential
entries.title: codifferential and normalized tensor inner product
entries.body:
When $M$ carries a Riemannian metric $g$, the \emph{codifferential}
  $\delta^k:\Omega^{k+1}(M)\to\Omega^k(M)$ is the adjoint $\nabla^*$ of $d^k$
  characterized by
  \[
    \int_Mg(\delta^k\omega_1,\omega_2)\,\mathrm{vol}=\int_Mg(\omega_1,d^k\omega_2)\,\mathrm{vol}.
  \]
  For tensors of a fixed type one uses the normalized inner product
  $(T_1,T_2)=\tfrac1{\mathrm{vol}\,M}\int_Mg(T_1,T_2)\,\mathrm{vol}$; $\delta$ is also the
  adjoint of $d$ with respect to this normalized pairing.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-hodge-laplacian
entries.title: Hodge Laplacian
entries.body:
The \emph{Laplacian on forms}, or \emph{Hodge Laplacian}, is
  $\square:\Omega^k(M)\to\Omega^k(M)$, $\square\omega=(d\delta+\delta d)\omega$.
  On functions $\square$ is the negative of the ordinary Laplacian $\Delta$,
  hence the different symbol.

deps.title: codifferential and normalized tensor inner product
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codifferential

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-harmonic-iff-closed-coclosed
entries.title: Lemma 9.1.1
entries.body:
$\square\omega=0$ if and only if $d\omega=0$ and $\delta\omega=0$. In
  particular $\omega=0$ if $\square\omega=0$ and $\omega=d\theta$ for some
  $\theta$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-hodge-cohomology
entries.title: Hodge cohomology
entries.body:
The \emph{Hodge cohomology} of $(M,g)$ is
  $\mathcal{H}^k(M)=\{\omega\in\Omega^k(M)\mid\square\omega=0\}$, the space of
  \emph{harmonic $k$-forms}.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-hodge-theorem
entries.title: Hodge, 1935
entries.body:
On a closed oriented Riemannian manifold the natural inclusion
  $\mathcal{H}^k(M)\to H^k(M)$ is an isomorphism.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-vector-field-1form-duality
entries.title: Proposition 9.2.1

deps.title: codifferential and normalized tensor inner product
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codifferential

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-bochner-formula-1forms
entries.title: Proposition 9.2.2
entries.body:
Let $X$ be a vector field with $\nabla X$ symmetric (equivalently the dual
  $1$-form is closed), $f=\tfrac12|X|^2$, and suppose $X=\nabla u$ near $p$. Then
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item $\nabla f=\nabla_XX$.
    \item $\operatorname{Hess}f(V,V)=\operatorname{Hess}^2u(V,V)+(\nabla_X\operatorname{Hess}u)(V,V)+R(V,X,X,V)
      =|\nabla_VX|^2+g(\nabla^2_XX,V)+R(V,X,X,V)$.
    \item $\Delta f=|\operatorname{Hess}u|^2+D_X\Delta u+\operatorname{Ric}(X,X)
      =|\nabla X|^2+D_X\operatorname{div}X+\operatorname{Ric}(X,X)$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-bochner-vanishing-1forms
entries.title: Theorem 9.2.3 (Bochner, 1948)
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is compact and $\operatorname{Ric}\ge0$, then every harmonic $1$-form is
  parallel.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch9-harmonic-1forms-vanish-positive-ricci
entries.title: Corollary 9.2.4
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is as in \cref{thm:pet-ch9-bochner-vanishing-1forms} and moreover
  $\operatorname{Ric}$ is positive at one point, then every harmonic $1$-form vanishes
  identically.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch9-betti1-bound-flat-torus
entries.title: Corollary 9.2.5
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is compact with $\operatorname{Ric}\ge0$, then $b_1(M)\le n=\dim M$, with
  equality if and only if $(M,g)$ is a flat torus.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-li-dimension-estimate
entries.title: Lemma 9.2.6 (P.\ Li)
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be compact and $E\to M$ a vector bundle of fibre dimension $m$
  with a smoothly varying fibre inner product. For $s\in\Gamma(E)$ set
  $\|s\|_\infty=\max_{x\in M}|s(x)|$ and $\|s\|_p=\big(\tfrac1{\mathrm{vol}\,M}
  \int_M|s|^p\,\mathrm{vol}\big)^{1/p}$. For a finite-dimensional $V\subset\Gamma(E)$
  put $C(V)=\max_{s\in V\setminus\{0\}}\|s\|_\infty/\|s\|_2$. Then
  $\dim V\le m\cdot C(V)$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-moser-iteration
entries.title: Theorem 9.2.7 (Moser iteration)
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be compact and suppose $\|u\|_{2\nu}\le S\|\nabla u\|_2+\|u\|_2$
  for all smooth $u$ on $M$, with $\nu>1$. If $f:M\to[0,\infty)$ is continuous,
  smooth on $\{f>0\}$, and $\Delta f\ge-\lambda f$, then
  \[
    \|f\|_\infty\le\exp\Big(\frac{S\sqrt{\lambda\nu}}{\sqrt{\nu-1}}\Big)\|f\|_2.
  \]

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-kato-inequality
entries.title: Kato's inequality
entries.body:
For a tensor field $T$ with $f=|T|$, smooth away from $\{T=0\}$, one has
  $df\le|\nabla T|$ pointwise wherever $f$ is smooth.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-gromov-gallot-betti1-estimate
entries.title: Theorem 9.2.8 (Gromov, 1980 and Gallot, 1981)
entries.body:
If $M$ is a compact Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\operatorname{Ric}\ge(n-1)k$ and
  $\operatorname{diam}(M)\le D$, there is a function $C(n,kD^2)$ with
  $b_1(M)\le C(n,kD^2)$ and $\lim_{\varepsilon\to0}C(n,\varepsilon)=n$. In
  particular there is $\varepsilon(n)>0$ so that $kD^2\ge-\varepsilon(n)$
  implies $b_1(M)\le n$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-vanishing-connection-laplacian
entries.title: Proposition 9.3.1
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be Riemannian and $T\in\Gamma(E)$ with $g(\nabla^*\nabla T,T)\le0$.
  If $|T|$ attains a maximum, then $T$ is parallel.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-gallot-connection-laplacian-estimate
entries.title: Theorem 9.3.2 (Gallot, 1981)
entries.body:
Assume $(M,g)$ is compact and satisfies the hypothesis of
  \cref{thm:pet-ch9-moser-iteration}. Let $V\subset\Gamma(E)$ be finite
  dimensional. If $g(\nabla^*\nabla T,T)\le\lambda|T|^2$ for all $T\in V$, then
  \[
    \dim V\le m\cdot\exp\Big(\frac{S\sqrt\lambda}{\sqrt{\nu-1}}\Big).
  \]

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-weitzenbock-curvature-operator
entries.title: Weitzenböck curvature operator
entries.body:
For a $(0,k)$-tensor $T$ the \emph{Weitzenböck curvature} of $T$ is
  \[
    \operatorname{Ric}(T)(X_1,\dots,X_k)=\sum_j\big(R(e_j,X_i)T\big)(X_1,\dots,e_j,\dots,X_k)
  \]
  (sum over $i$ implicit in the slot notation, and over an orthonormal frame
  $e_j$). For a $1$-form $\omega$ dual to a vector field $X$, $\operatorname{Ric}(\omega)(X')
  =\sum_j(R(e_j,X')\omega)(e_j)=-\omega\big(\sum_jR(e_j,X')e_j\big)=\omega(\operatorname{Ric}(X'))$,
  recovering the ordinary Ricci tensor; the operator is often written $W$, but
  $\operatorname{Ric}$ is used here to stress this compatibility.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-lichnerowicz-laplacian
entries.title: Lichnerowicz Laplacian
entries.body:
For a constant $c>0$, the \emph{Lichnerowicz Laplacian} on sections of $E$ is
  $\Delta_LT=\nabla^*\nabla T+c\operatorname{Ric}(T)$. The Hodge Laplacian on forms is of
  this type with $c=1$; symmetric $(0,2)$-tensors and the curvature tensor use
  $c=\tfrac12$.

entries.label: conv:pet-ch9-so-metric-convention
entries.title: metric on $\mathfrak{so}(T_pM)$
entries.body:
For each $X,Y\in T_pM$ the map $R_{X,Y}:T_pM\to T_pM$ is skew-symmetric,
  hence decomposes in an orthonormal basis $\Xi_\sigma$ of
  $\mathfrak{so}(T_pM)$. Since an orthonormal $v,w$ give a unit bivector
  $v\wedge w\in\Lambda^2T_pM$ while the corresponding rotation by $\pi/2$ has
  Euclidean operator norm $\sqrt2$, $\mathfrak{so}(T_pM)$ is endowed with the
  metric transported from $\Lambda^2T_pM$ under $v\wedge w\leftrightarrow$
  (rotation by $\pi/2$ in $\operatorname{span}\{v,w\}$). With the curvature operator
  $\mathfrak{R}$ on $\Lambda^2T_pM\cong\mathfrak{so}(T_pM)$ this gives
  \[
    R_{X,Y}=\sum_\sigma g(R_{X,Y},\Xi_\sigma)\Xi_\sigma
    =\sum_\sigma g(\mathfrak{R}(X\wedge Y),\Xi_\sigma)\Xi_\sigma
    =\sum_\sigma g(\mathfrak{R}(\Xi_\sigma),X\wedge Y)\Xi_\sigma
    =-\sum_\sigma g(R(\Xi_\sigma)X,Y)\Xi_\sigma.
  \]

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-ric-curvature-operator-formula
entries.title: Lemma 9.3.3
entries.body:
For any $(0,k)$-tensor $T$,
  \[
    \operatorname{Ric}(T)=-\sum_\sigma R(\Xi_\sigma)(\Xi_\sigma T),\qquad
    \Delta_LT=\nabla^*\nabla T-c\sum_\sigma R(\Xi_\sigma)(\Xi_\sigma T).
  \]
  Moreover $\operatorname{Ric}$ is self-adjoint.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch9-ric-nonneg-curvature-operator
entries.title: Corollary 9.3.4
entries.body:
If $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$, then $g(\operatorname{Ric}(T),T)\ge0$ for every tensor $T$. More
  generally, if $\mathfrak{R}\ge k$ with $k<0$, then $g(\operatorname{Ric}(T),T)\ge kC|T|^2$
  for a constant $C>0$ depending only on the tensor type.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-lichnerowicz-dimension-estimate
entries.title: Theorem 9.3.5
entries.body:
If $\mathfrak{R}\ge k$ and $\operatorname{diam}(M)\le D$, the dimension of
  $V=\{T\in\Gamma(E)\mid\Delta_LT=\nabla^*\nabla T+c\operatorname{Ric}(T)=0\}$ is bounded by
  \[
    m\cdot\exp\Big(D\cdot C(n,kD^2)\frac{\sqrt{kcC\nu}}{\sqrt{\nu-1}}\Big),
  \]
  and when $k=0$ every $T\in V$ is parallel.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-weitzenbock-hodge-laplacian
entries.title: Theorem 9.4.1 (Weitzenböck, 1923)
entries.body:
The Hodge Laplacian is the Lichnerowicz Laplacian with $c=1$:
  \[
    \square\omega=(d\delta+\delta d)\omega=\nabla^*\nabla\omega+\operatorname{Ric}(\omega).
  \]

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-curvature-tensor-lichnerowicz
entries.title: Theorem 9.4.2
entries.body:
The curvature tensor $R$ of a Riemannian manifold satisfies
   egin{align*}
    (
abla^*
abla R)(X,Y,Z,W)+	frac12\operatorname{Ric}(R)(X,Y,Z,W)
    &=	frac12(
abla_X
abla^*R)(Y,Z,W)-	frac12(
abla_Y
abla^*R)(X,Z,W)\
    &\quad+	frac12(
abla_Z
abla^*R)(W,X,Y)-	frac12(
abla_W
abla^*R)(Z,X,Y).
  \end{align*}
  In particular $\Delta_LR=
abla^*
abla R+	frac12\operatorname{Ric}(R)$ vanishes whenever
  $
abla^*R=0$, i.e.\ $R$ is a Lichnerowicz-harmonic tensor ($c=	frac12$)
  precisely when it is divergence free.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative
entries.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
entries.body:
For a symmetric $(0,2)$-tensor $h$ with associated $(1,1)$-tensor $H$
  ($h(z,w)=g(H(z),w)$), recalling $(d^
abla H)(X,Y)=(
abla_XH)(Y)-(
abla_YH)(X)$
  for $(1,1)$-tensors, define
  \[
    d^
abla h(X,Y,Z)=(
abla_Xh)(Y,Z)-(
abla_Yh)(X,Z).
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-ricci-codazzi-identity
entries.title: Codazzi identity for the Ricci tensor
entries.body:
For the second fundamental form $\mathrm{II}$ of an immersed hypersurface
  $M^n\to\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$, the Codazzi--Mainardi equations read $d^\nabla\mathrm{II}=0$.
  For the Ricci tensor, Petersen's exercise 3.4.8 gives
  $(d^\nabla\operatorname{Ric})(X,Y,Z)=(\nabla^*R)(Z,X,Y)$; an analogous identity relates
  the Schouten and Weyl tensors (Petersen, exercise 3.4.26).

deps.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-symmetric-tensor-lichnerowicz-identity
entries.title: Theorem 9.4.3
entries.body:
Any symmetric $(0,2)$-tensor $h$ on a Riemannian manifold satisfies
  \[
    (
abla_X
abla^*h)(X)+(
abla^*d^
abla h)(X,X)
    =(
abla^*
abla h)(X,X)+	frac12(\operatorname{Ric}(h))(X,X).
  \]

deps.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-harmonic-tensor
entries.title: Codazzi and harmonic symmetric tensors
entries.body:
A symmetric $(0,2)$-tensor $h$ is a \emph{Codazzi tensor} if $d^\nabla h=0$,
  and \emph{harmonic} if in addition it is divergence free ($\nabla^*h=0$).

deps.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-harmonic-iff-codazzi-constant-trace
entries.title: Proposition 9.4.4
entries.body:
A symmetric $(0,2)$-tensor is harmonic if and only if it is a Codazzi tensor
  of constant trace.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch9-ricci-harmonic-iff-curvature-harmonic
entries.title: Corollary 9.4.5
entries.body:
The Ricci tensor is harmonic if and only if the curvature tensor is
  harmonic (i.e.\ $\nabla^*R=0$).

deps.title: Codazzi identity for the Ricci tensor
deps.label: rem:pet-ch9-ricci-codazzi-identity

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-nonneg-curvature-operator-parallel-forms
entries.title: Theorem 9.4.6 (D.\ Meyer, 1971, D.\ Meyer--Gallot, 1975, and Gallot, 1981)
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a closed Riemannian $n$-manifold. If $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$, then
  every harmonic form is parallel. If $\mathfrak{R}>0$, the only parallel
  $l$-forms occur for $l=0,n$. Finally, if $\mathfrak{R}\ge k$ and
  $\operatorname{diam}(M)\le D$,
  \[
    b_l(M)\le\binom{n}{l}\exp\big(D\cdot C(n,kD^2)\sqrt{-kC}\big).
  \]

deps.title: Theorem 9.4.6 (D.\ Meyer, 1971, D.\ Meyer--Gallot, 1975, and Gallot, 1981)
deps.label: thm:pet-ch9-nonneg-curvature-operator-parallel-forms

entries.label: ex:pet-ch9-cp2-connect-sum-nonneg-curvature
entries.title: Example 9.4.7
entries.body:
Let $S^1$ act on $S^3\subset\mathbb{C}^2$ by the Hopf action and on $S^2$ by
  rotation through total angle $2\pi k$; the quotient of $S^2\times S^3$ by
  the diagonal action is $S^2\times S^2$ if $k$ is even and
  $\mathbb{CP}^2\#\mathbb{CP}^2$ if $k$ is odd, and by O'Neill's formula it carries
  nonnegative sectional curvature in either case. By the classification of
  manifolds with $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$ (Petersen, theorem 10.3.7), the only simply
  connected manifolds with $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$ are (topologically) $S^2\times S^2$,
  $S^4$, or $\mathbb{CP}^2$, so this $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$-classification is strictly
  finer than the classification of $\sec\ge0$ manifolds; these examples were
  originally due to Cheeger, via a different construction.

deps.title: Theorem 9.4.6 (D.\ Meyer, 1971, D.\ Meyer--Gallot, 1975, and Gallot, 1981)
deps.label: thm:pet-ch9-nonneg-curvature-operator-parallel-forms

entries.label: thm:pet-ch9-tachibana-parallel-curvature
entries.title: Theorem 9.4.8 (Tachibana, 1974)
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be closed. If $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$ and $\nabla^*R=0$, then $\nabla R=0$.
  If moreover $\mathfrak{R}>0$, then $(M,g)$ has constant curvature.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-tensor-lambda2-valued
entries.title: $\Lambda^2TM$-valued tensor $\hat T$
entries.body:
A $(0,k)$-tensor $T$ determines $\hat T$, valued in $\Lambda^2TM\cong
  \mathfrak{so}(TM)$, implicitly by
  \[
    g\big(L,\hat T(X_1,\dots,X_k)\big)=(LT)(X_1,\dots,X_k)\quad\text{for all }
    L\in\mathfrak{so}(TM).
  \]

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-ric-curvature-operator-hat-formula
entries.title: Lemma 9.4.9
entries.body: For all $(0,k)$-tensors $T,S$, $g(\operatorname{Ric}(T),S)=g(\mathfrak{R}(\hat T),\hat S)$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-1form-hat-ricci
entries.title: Proposition 9.4.10
entries.body:
If $\omega$ is a $1$-form dual to the vector field $X$, then
  $\hat\omega(Z)=X\wedge Z$ and $g(\mathfrak{R}(\hat\omega),\hat\omega)=\operatorname{Ric}(X,X)$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-pform-hat-formula
entries.title: $p$-form generalization
entries.body:
More generally, if $\omega$ is a $p$-form and $\Omega$ is the
  $(p-1)$-form-valued map with $g(\Omega(X_1,\dots,X_{p-1}),X_p)=
  \omega(X_1,\dots,X_p)$, then
  \[
    \hat\omega(X_1,\dots,X_p)=\sum_{i=1}^p(-1)^{p-i}X_i\wedge
    \Omega(X_1,\dots,\hat X_i,\dots,X_p),
  \]
  and $\hat\omega$ vanishes only where $\omega$ does.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-02tensor-hat-formula
entries.title: Proposition 9.4.11
entries.body:
With $h,H,H^*$ as above, $\hat h(z,w)=H(z)\wedge w-z\wedge H^*(w)$, and
  $\hat h=0$ if and only if $h=\lambda g$ for a scalar $\lambda$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch9-complex-sectional-curvature
entries.title: complex sectional curvature
entries.body:
For $z,w\in T_pM\otimes\mathbb{C}$, the \emph{complex sectional curvature} is
  $g(\mathfrak{R}(z\wedge w),\bar z\wedge\bar w)$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch9-complex-sectional-curvature-real-formula
entries.title: real expansion of the complex sectional curvature
entries.body:
Writing $z=x+iy$, $w=u+iv$ with $x,y,u,v\in T_pM$,
  \begin{align*}
    g(\mathfrak{R}(z\wedge w),\bar z\wedge\bar w)
    &=R(x,u,u,x)+R(y,v,v,y)+R(x,v,v,x)+R(y,u,u,y)+2R(y,x,v,u).
  \end{align*}
  In particular this quantity is nonnegative whenever $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$, so
  nonnegative complex sectional curvature is a weaker hypothesis than
  $\mathfrak{R}\ge0$ but a stronger one than nonnegative sectional curvature.

deps.title: real expansion of the complex sectional curvature
deps.label: lem:pet-ch9-complex-sectional-curvature-real-formula

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-complex-sectional-curvature-special-cases
entries.title: special cases of the complex sectional curvature
entries.body:
Depending on $\dim_{\mathbb{R}}\operatorname{span}\{x,y,u,v\}$: if $y=v=0$ the complex
  sectional curvature is the ordinary sectional curvature $R(x,u,u,x)$; if
  $x,y,u,v$ are orthonormal it is called the \emph{isotropic curvature}; and if
  $u=v$ it reduces to $2R(x,u,u,x)+2R(y,u,u,y)$, called the \emph{second Ricci
  curvature} when $x,y,u$ are orthonormal.

deps.title: real expansion of the complex sectional curvature
deps.label: lem:pet-ch9-complex-sectional-curvature-real-formula

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-normal-tensor-nonneg-complex-sectional
entries.title: Proposition 9.4.12
entries.body:
Let $h$ be a $(0,2)$-tensor with $H$ normal. If all complex sectional
  curvatures are nonnegative, then $g(\mathfrak{R}(\hat h),\hat h)\ge0$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch9-general-tensor-nonneg-complex-sectional
entries.title: Proposition 9.4.13
entries.body:
$g(\mathfrak{R}(\hat h),\hat h)\ge0$ for every $(0,2)$-tensor $h$ on $T_pM$, as
  soon as every complex sectional curvature on $T_pM$ is nonnegative.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-1
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.1
entries.body:
Suppose $(M^n,g)$ is compact with $b_1=k$. If $\operatorname{Ric}\ge0$, show that the
  universal cover splits, $(\tilde M,g)=(N,h)\times(\mathbb{R}^k,g_{\mathbb{R}^k})$. Give an
  example where $b_1<n$ and $(\tilde M,g)=(\mathbb{R}^n,g_{\mathbb{R}^n})$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-2
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.2
entries.body:
Show directly that if $E_i$ is an orthonormal frame and $v\mapsto
abla_vX$
  is symmetric, then $\sum_ig(
abla_{E_i}X,
abla_XE_i)=0$, without assuming
  the $E_i$ are parallel along $X$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-3
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.3
entries.body:
Show that for an oriented Riemannian manifold,
  $(\operatorname{im}d^{k-1})^\perp=\ker\delta^{k-1}$ and $\operatorname{im}d^{k-1}\subset
  (\ker\delta^{k-1})^\perp$ in $\Omega^k(M)$.

deps.title: codifferential and normalized tensor inner product
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codifferential

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-4
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.4
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)$ be compact and $E	o M$ a tensor bundle. Let $W^{k,2}(E)$ be the
  Hilbert space completion of $\Gamma(E)$ with square norm
  $\sum_{i=0}^k\|
abla^iT\|_2^2$, so that $T\in W^{1,2}(E)$ has an $L^2$
  derivative $
abla T$ satisfying $(
abla T,
abla S)=(T,
abla^*
abla S)$ for
  all $S\in\Gamma(E)$; $\Gamma(E)\subset W^{k,2}(E)$ is dense, the Sobolev
  inequality gives $ igcap_kW^{k,2}(E)= igcap_kW^{k,p}(E)$ for all $p$, and
  the techniques of section 7.1.5 show a tensor in $W^{1,p}$ is H"older
  continuous for $p>n$, so $\Gamma(E)= igcap_kW^{k,2}(E)$.
   egin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show that for all $T\in\Gamma(E)$ there is a commutation
      relation $
abla^*
abla(
abla^kT)-
abla^k(
abla^*
abla T)
      =\sum_{i=0}^kC_i^k(
abla^{k-1}R\otimes
abla^iT)$ for suitable
      contractions $C_i^k$.
    \item[(2)] If $T\in W^{1,2}(E)$ and $T'\in W^{k,2}(E)$ satisfy
      $(T',S)=(
abla T,
abla S)$ for all $S\in\Gamma(E)$ (i.e.\
      $
abla^*
abla T=T'$ weakly), show $T\in W^{k+1,2}(E)$, by defining weak
      derivatives $
abla^{l+1}T$ inductively via
      $(
abla^{l+1}T,
abla^{l+1}S)=(
abla^lT',
abla^lS)
      +\sum_{i=0}^l(C_i^l(
abla^{l-i}R\otimes
abla^iT),
abla^lS)$.
    \item[(3)] Conclude that if $T'\in\Gamma(E)$, then $T\in\Gamma(E)$ and
      $
abla^*
abla T=T'$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-5
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.5
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)$ be compact with $\operatorname{diam}M\le D$, $\operatorname{Ric}\ge k$, and $E\to M$ a
  tensor bundle of fibre dimension $m$ with Lichnerowicz Laplacian $\Delta_L$.
  Establish the spectral theorem for $\Delta_L$ and conclude the orthogonal
  decomposition $\Gamma(E)=\ker\Delta_L\oplus\operatorname{im}\Delta_L$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Using the completion $W^{1,2}(E)$ of Exercise 9.6.4, show that
      $(\Delta_LT,S)=(\nabla T,\nabla S)+c(\operatorname{Ric}(T),S)$ is symmetric and
      well-defined for $T,S\in W^{1,2}(E)$.
    \item[(2)] Show $\inf_{T\in W^{1,2},\|T\|_2=1}(\Delta_LT,T)>cCk$ for $k\le0$,
      with $C$ the constant of Corollary 9.3.4.
    \item[(3)] Show a sequence $T_i\subset W^{1,2}(E)$ with $\|T_i\|_2=1$ and
      $(\Delta_LT_i,T_i)$ bounded has an $L^2$-convergent subsequence that is
      also weakly convergent in $W^{1,2}(E)$ (use theorem 7.1.18).
    \item[(4)] For a closed $\Delta_L$-invariant subspace $V\subset W^{1,2}(E)$,
      show the infimum $\lambda=\inf_{T\in V,\|T\|_2=1}(\Delta_LT,T)$ is
      achieved by some $T\in V$, and using (1)–(3) of Exercise 9.6.4 that
      $T\in\Gamma(E)$ and $\Delta_LT=\lambda T$.
    \item[(5)] For a finite-dimensional $V\subset\Gamma(E)$ spanned by
      eigentensors $\Delta_LT_i=\lambda_iT_i$, show
      $\dim V\le mC(n,\max_i\lambda_i,c,D^2k)$.
    \item[(6)] Show all eigenspaces of $\Delta_L$ are finite dimensional and
      the eigenvalues form a discrete set $\lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots\to\infty$.
    \item[(7)] Show the eigenspaces are orthogonal with dense direct sum in
      $\Gamma(E)$.
    \item[(8)] Show $\Gamma(E)=\ker\Delta_L\oplus\operatorname{im}\Delta_L$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-6
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.6
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be $n$-dimensional and isometric to Euclidean space outside a
  compact $K\subset M$, i.e.\ $M-K$ is isometric to $\mathbb{R}^n-C$ for compact
  $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$. If $\operatorname{Ric}_g\ge0$, show $M=\mathbb{R}^n$. (Hint: find a metric on
  the $n$-torus isometric to a neighborhood of $K$ and otherwise flat, or show
  a parallel $1$-form on $\mathbb{R}^n-C$ extends to a harmonic $1$-form on $M$ that
  Bochner's formula forces to be parallel.)

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-7
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.7
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be Einstein. Show that all harmonic $1$-forms are eigenforms for
  the connection Laplacian $\nabla^*\nabla$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-8
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.8
entries.body:
Given vector fields $X,Y$ on $(M,g)$ with $\nabla X,\nabla Y$ symmetric,
  develop Bochner formulas for $\operatorname{Hess}\tfrac12g(X,Y)$ and
  $\Delta\tfrac12g(X,Y)$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-9
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.9
entries.body:
For tensors $s_1,s_2$ of the same type, show, in analogy with
  $\Delta	frac12|s|^2=|
abla s|^2-g(
abla^*
abla s,s)$, that
  \[
    \Delta g(s_1,s_2)=2g(
abla s_1,
abla s_2)+g(
abla^*
abla s_1,s_2)
    +g(s_1,
abla^*
abla s_2).
  \]
  Use this on forms to develop Bochner formulas for inner products of such
  sections. More generally, for the $1$-form $\omega(v)=g(
abla_vs_1,s_2)$
  (representing half the differential of $g(s_1,s_2)$), show
  \[
    -\delta\omega=g(
abla s_1,
abla s_2)-g(
abla^*
abla s_1,s_2),
  \]
  \[
    d\omega(X,Y)=g(R(X,Y)s_1,s_2)-g(
abla_Xs_1,
abla_Ys_2)+g(
abla_Ys_1,
abla_Xs_2).
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-10
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.10
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be $n$-dimensional.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show $L\omega=0$ if $L$ is skew-symmetric and $\omega$ is an
      $n$-form.
    \item[(2)] When $n=2$, show $LR=0$ for all skew-symmetric $L$.
    \item[(3)] For general $L$, show $L\,\mathrm{vol}=\operatorname{tr}(L)\,\mathrm{vol}$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-11
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.11 (Simons)
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be compact with a symmetric Codazzi tensor $h$ of constant
  trace.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] If $\sec\ge0$, show $\nabla h=0$.
    \item[(2)] If $\sec>0$, show $h=c\cdot g$ for a constant $c$.
    \item[(3)] If the Gauss equations $R(X,Y,Z,W)=h(X,W)h(Y,Z)-h(X,Z)h(Y,W)$
      hold and $\operatorname{tr}h=0$, show
      $\Delta\tfrac12|h|^2\ge|\nabla h|^2-|h|^4$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-12
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.12
entries.body:
Let $(M^n,g)\hookrightarrow\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ be an isometric immersion.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show the second fundamental form $\mathrm{II}$ is a Codazzi
      tensor.
    \item[(2)] Show Liebmann's theorem: if $(M,g)$ has constant mean curvature
      and nonnegative second fundamental form, then $(M,g)$ is a constant
      curvature sphere. (Wente exhibits immersed tori of constant mean
      curvature, so some positivity hypothesis is necessary.)
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-13
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.13 (Berger)
entries.body:
Show that a compact manifold with harmonic curvature and nonnegative
  sectional curvature has parallel Ricci curvature.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-14
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.14
entries.body:
Let $K$ be a Killing field on a closed $(M,g)$ and $\omega$ a harmonic form.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show $\mathcal{L}_K\omega=0$ (hint: show $\mathcal{L}_K\omega$
      is also harmonic).
    \item[(2)] Show $i_K\omega$ is closed but not necessarily harmonic.
    \item[(3)] Show all harmonic forms are invariant under $\operatorname{Iso}_0(M)$.
    \item[(4)] Give an example where a harmonic form is not invariant under
      all of $\operatorname{Iso}(M)$.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-15
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.15
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a closed K\"ahler manifold with K\"ahler form $\omega$ (a
  parallel nondegenerate $2$-form). Show $\omega^k=\omega\wedge\cdots\wedge
  \omega$ ($k$ times) is closed but not exact, by showing
  $\omega^{\dim M/2}$ is proportional to the volume form; conclude that no
  even homology group vanishes.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-16
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.16
entries.body:
Let $E\to M$ be a tensor bundle.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Let $\Omega^p(M,E)$ be the alternating $p$-linear maps
      $TM\to E$ (so $\Omega^0(M,E)=\Gamma(E)$). Show $\Omega^*(M)$ acts
      naturally on both sides of $\Omega^*(M,E)$ by wedge product.
    \item[(2)] Show there is a natural wedge product
      $\Omega^p(M,\operatorname{Hom}(E,E))\times\Omega^q(M,E)\to\Omega^{p+q}(M,E)$.
    \item[(3)] Show there is a connection-dependent exterior derivative
      $d^\nabla:\Omega^p(M,E)\to\Omega^{p+1}(M,E)$ satisfying the Leibniz rule
      for the above wedge products, with $d^\nabla s=\nabla s$ for
      $s\in\Gamma(E)$.
    \item[(4)] Viewing $R(X,Y)s\in\Omega^2(M,\operatorname{Hom}(E,E))$, show
      $(d^\nabla\circ d^\nabla)(s)=R\wedge s$ for $s\in\Omega^p(M,E)$, and that
      the second Bianchi identity reads $d^\nabla R=0$.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: the exterior derivative $d^\nabla h$ of a symmetric tensor
deps.label: def:pet-ch9-codazzi-exterior-derivative

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-17
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.17
entries.body:
Let $E=TM$ in Exercise 9.6.16, so $\Omega^1(M,TM)=\operatorname{Hom}(TM,TM)$ consists of
  all $(1,1)$-tensors.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item[(1)] Show $d^\nabla s=0$ if and only if $s$ is a Codazzi tensor.
    \item[(2)] The chapter suggests that whenever $s\in\Omega^p(M,E)$ satisfies
      $d^\nabla s=0$, there is a Bochner-type formula for $s$, and when $s$ is
      also divergence free with some curvature nonnegative, $s$ should be
      parallel. Develop such a theory in this generality.
    \item[(3)] Show that for a vector field $X$, $\nabla X$ is Codazzi if and
      only if $R(\cdot,\cdot)X=0$. Give an example where $\nabla X$ is Codazzi
      but $X$ is not parallel. Can a Bochner-type formula be established for
      exact tensors $\nabla X=d^\nabla X$ even when not closed?
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch9-exercise-9-6-18
entries.title: Exercise 9.6.18 (Thomas)
entries.body:
Show that in dimension $n\ge3$ the Gauss equations $\mathfrak{R}=S\wedge S$
  imply the Codazzi equations $d^\nabla S=0$, provided $\det S\ne0$. (Hint: use
  the second Bianchi identity, and study the linear map
  $\operatorname{Hom}(\Lambda^2V,V)\to\operatorname{Hom}(\Lambda^3V,\Lambda^2V)$, $T\mapsto T\wedge S$,
  for a linear map $S:V\to V$; this map is injective only when
  $\operatorname{rank}S\ge4$.)

entries.label: def:pet-ch10-symmetric-space
entries.title: symmetric space
entries.body:
A Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$ is a \emph{symmetric space} if for each
  $p\in M$ the isotropy group $\mathrm{Iso}_p$ contains an isometry $A_p$ (the
  \emph{point symmetry} at $p$) such that $DA_p:T_pM\to T_pM$ is the antipodal
  map $-I$. Since isometries preserve geodesics, any geodesic $c(t)$ with
  $c(0)=p$ satisfies $A_p(c(t))=c(-t)$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch10-symmetric-homogeneous
entries.title: symmetric spaces are homogeneous, hence complete
entries.body: A symmetric space is homogeneous, and consequently complete.

deps.title: symmetric spaces are homogeneous, hence complete
deps.label: prop:pet-ch10-symmetric-homogeneous

entries.label: rem:pet-ch10-single-point-symmetry
entries.title: a symmetry at one point suffices; biinvariant Lie groups
entries.body:
A homogeneous space $G/H=\mathrm{Iso}/\mathrm{Iso}_p$ is symmetric as soon as
  the point symmetry $A_p$ exists at a single point $p$: for $q=h\cdot p$ with
  $h\in\mathrm{Iso}$, $A_q:=h\circ A_p\circ h^{-1}$ is a point symmetry at $q$.
  In particular, every Lie group $G$ with a biinvariant metric is a symmetric
  space, since $g\mapsto g^{-1}$ is the desired point symmetry at the
  identity element.

entries.label: def:pet-ch10-symmetric-space-tables
entries.title: Tables 10.1--10.4 — compact groups, their noncompact duals, and the classical rank-one symmetric spaces
entries.body:
The following families of homogeneous spaces are symmetric, always occurring
  in dual pairs of compact and noncompact spaces (there are many further
  families and several exceptional examples).
  \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{c|c|c}
      \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{Table 10.1 Compact Groups}}\\
      group & rank & dim\\\hline
      $\mathrm{SU}(n+1)$ & $n$ & $n(n+2)$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(2n+1)$ & $n$ & $n(2n+1)$\\
      $\mathrm{Sp}(n)$ & $n$ & $n(2n+1)$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(2n)$ & $n$ & $n(2n-1)$
    \end{tabular}
    \qquad
    \begin{tabular}{c|c|c}
      \multicolumn{3}{c}{\textbf{Table 10.2 Noncompact Analogues}}\\
      (complexified group)/group & rank & dim\\\hline
      $\mathrm{SL}(n{+}1,\mathbb C)/\mathrm{SU}(n{+}1)$ & $n$ & $n(n+2)$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(2n{+}1,\mathbb C)/\mathrm{SO}(2n{+}1)$ & $n$ & $n(2n+1)$\\
      $\mathrm{Sp}(n,\mathbb C)/\mathrm{Sp}(n)$ & $n$ & $n(2n+1)$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(2n,\mathbb C)/\mathrm{SO}(2n)$ & $n$ & $n(2n-1)$
    \end{tabular}
  \end{center}
  \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{l|l|c|c|l}
      \multicolumn{5}{c}{\textbf{Table 10.3 Compact Homogeneous Spaces}}\\
      $\mathrm{Iso}$ & $\mathrm{Iso}_p$ & dim & rank & description\\\hline
      $\mathrm{SO}(n{+}1)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ & $n$ & $1$ & sphere\\
      $\mathrm O(n{+}1)$ & $\mathrm O(n)\times\{1,-1\}$ & $n$ & $1$ & $\mathbb{RP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm U(n{+}1)$ & $\mathrm U(n)\times\mathrm U(1)$ & $2n$ & $1$ & $\mathbb{CP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm{Sp}(n{+}1)$ & $\mathrm{Sp}(n)\times\mathrm{Sp}(1)$ & $4n$ & $1$ & $\mathbb{HP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm F_4$ & $\mathrm{Spin}(9)$ & $16$ & $1$ & $\mathbb{OP}^2$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(p{+}q)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(p)\times\mathrm{SO}(q)$ & $pq$ & $\min(p,q)$ & real Grassmannian\\
      $\mathrm{SU}(p{+}q)$ & $\mathrm S(\mathrm U(p)\times\mathrm U(q))$ & $2pq$ & $\min(p,q)$ & complex Grassmannian\\
      $\mathrm{SU}(n)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ & $\tfrac{n^2-1}2$ & $n-1$ & $\mathbb R^n$'s in $\mathbb C^n$
    \end{tabular}
  \end{center}
  \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{l|l|c|c|l}
      \multicolumn{5}{c}{\textbf{Table 10.4 Noncompact Homogeneous Spaces}}\\
      $\mathrm{Iso}$ & $\mathrm{Iso}_p$ & dim & rank & description\\\hline
      $\mathrm{SO}(n,1)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ & $n$ & $1$ & hyperbolic space\\
      $\mathrm O(n,1)$ & $\mathrm O(n)\times\{1,-1\}$ & $n$ & $1$ & hyperbolic $\mathbb{RP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm U(n,1)$ & $\mathrm U(n)\times\mathrm U(1)$ & $2n$ & $1$ & hyperbolic $\mathbb{CP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm{Sp}(n,1)$ & $\mathrm{Sp}(n)\times\mathrm{Sp}(1)$ & $4n$ & $1$ & hyperbolic $\mathbb{HP}^n$\\
      $\mathrm F_4^{-20}$ & $\mathrm{Spin}(9)$ & $16$ & $1$ & hyperbolic $\mathbb{OP}^2$\\
      $\mathrm{SO}(p,q)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(p)\times\mathrm{SO}(q)$ & $pq$ & $\min(p,q)$ & hyperbolic Grassmannian\\
      $\mathrm{SU}(p,q)$ & $\mathrm S(\mathrm U(p)\times\mathrm U(q))$ & $2pq$ & $\min(p,q)$ & complex hyperbolic Grassmannian\\
      $\mathrm{SL}(n,\mathbb R)$ & $\mathrm{SO}(n)$ & $\tfrac{n^2-1}2$ & $n-1$ & Euclidean structures on $\mathbb R^n$
    \end{tabular}
  \end{center}
  Here $\mathrm{Spin}(n)$ is the universal double cover of $\mathrm{SO}(n)$,
  $n>2$, with the low-dimensional identities
  $\mathrm{SO}(2)=\mathrm U(1)$, $\mathrm{Spin}(3)=\mathrm{SU}(2)=\mathrm{Sp}(1)$,
  $\mathrm{Spin}(4)=\mathrm{Spin}(3)\times\mathrm{Spin}(3)$. By O'Neill's formula
  (Petersen's Theorem 4.5.3, outside this chapter) all the compact examples
  have $\sec\ge0$, and all the projective spaces (compact and noncompact) have
  quarter-pinched metrics, i.e.\ the ratio of smallest to largest sectional
  curvature is $\tfrac14$.
