entries.label: rem:pet-ch10-exercise-bracket-tp-isop
entries.title: Exercise 10.5.25 — $[t_p,\mathrm{iso}_p]\subset t_p$
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a symmetric space. Show that if $X\in t_p$ and $Y\in\mathrm{iso}_p$,
  then $[X,Y]\in t_p$.

deps.title: Prop. 10.1.4 — the bracket of $\mathrm{iso}$ from the curvature
deps.label: prop:pet-ch10-lie-bracket-curvature

entries.label: rem:pet-ch10-exercise-curvature-ricci-bracket-formula
entries.title: Exercise 10.5.26 — curvature and Ricci tensor via the $L_X$ operators
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a symmetric space and $X,Y,Z\in t_p$. Show
  $R(X,Y)Z=[L_X,L_Y]Z$ and $\operatorname{Ric}(X,Y)=-\operatorname{tr}([L_X,L_Y])$, where
  $L_X:=\operatorname{ad}_X$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch10-exercise-parallel-form-holonomy-invariant
entries.title: Exercise 10.5.27 — parallel extension of a $p$-form via holonomy invariance
entries.body:
Consider a Riemannian manifold $M$ and a $p$-form $\omega$ on $T_pM$. Show
  that $\omega$ extends to a parallel form on $M$ if and only if $\omega$ is
  invariant under $\mathrm{Hol}_p(M)$.

deps.title: elementary properties of the holonomy group
deps.label: rem:pet-ch10-holonomy-properties

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-hausdorff-distance
entries.title: Hausdorff distance
entries.body:
For a metric space $(X,|\cdot|)$ and $A,B\subset X$, set
  $d(A,B)=\inf\{|ab|\mid a\in A,\,b\in B\}$,
  $B(A,\varepsilon)=\{x\in X\mid|xA|<\varepsilon\}$ (writing $|xA|=d(\{x\},A)$),
  and define the \emph{Hausdorff distance}
  \[
    d_H(A,B)=\inf\{\varepsilon\mid A\subset B(B,\varepsilon),\,B\subset B(A,\varepsilon)\}.
  \]
  Thus $d(A,B)$ is small when some points of $A,B$ are close, while
  $d_H(A,B)$ is small exactly when every point of $A$ is close to a point of
  $B$ and vice versa. The Hausdorff distance defines a metric on the compact
  subsets of $X$, and this collection is compact when $X$ is compact.

entries.label: conv:pet-ch11-compact-proper-spaces
entries.title: compact and proper metric spaces
entries.body:
Throughout, only compact or \emph{proper} metric spaces are considered:
  spaces whose distance function is proper, i.e.\ all closed balls are
  compact. Proper metric spaces are automatically separable, complete, and
  locally compact.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-gromov-hausdorff-distance
entries.title: admissible metric, Gromov--Hausdorff distance
entries.body:
For metric spaces $X,Y$, an \emph{admissible metric} on the disjoint union
  $X\cup Y$ is a metric that extends the given metrics on $X$ and on $Y$. The
  \emph{Gromov--Hausdorff distance} is
  \[
    \dGH(X,Y)=\inf\{d_H(X,Y)\mid\text{admissible metrics on }X\cup Y\}:
  \]
  one places a metric on $X\cup Y$ extending those on $X,Y$ so as to make $X$
  and $Y$ as Hausdorff-close as possible — equivalently, one tries to define
  distances between points of $X$ and $Y$ without violating the triangle
  inequality.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-gh-distance-radius
entries.title: Example 11.1.1 — distance to a point
entries.body:
If $Y$ is a one-point space, then
  $\dGH(X,Y)\le\operatorname{rad}X=\inf_{y}\sup_{x\in X}|xy|$, the radius of
  the smallest ball covering $X$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-gh-distance-diameter-bound
entries.title: Example 11.1.2 — diameter bound
entries.body:
If $\diam X,\diam Y\le D$, using $|xy|=D/2$ for all $x\in X$, $y\in Y$ as an
  admissible metric on $X\cup Y$ shows $\dGH(X,Y)\le D/2$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-gh-distance-zero-isometric
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.1.3 — distance zero implies isometric
entries.body:
If $X$ and $Y$ are compact metric spaces with $\dGH(X,Y)=0$, then $X$ and
  $Y$ are isometric.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-gh-pseudo-metric-quotient
entries.title: $(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$ as a metric space
entries.body:
Symmetry and the triangle inequality for $\dGH$ are straightforward, so
  $\dGH$ is a pseudo-metric on the collection $\mathcal{M}$ of compact metric
  spaces; by \cref{prop:pet-ch11-gh-distance-zero-isometric} the equivalence
  classes (up to isometry) form a genuine metric space $(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-epsilon-dense-approximation
entries.title: Example 11.1.4 — $\varepsilon$-dense finite approximation
entries.body:
Let $X$ be compact and $A\subset X$ finite with $d_H(A,X)\le\varepsilon$
  (metric on $A$ induced from $X$); such $A$ is \emph{$\varepsilon$-dense} in
  $X$. Then $\dGH(X,A)\le\varepsilon$; since $X$ is compact, finite
  $\varepsilon$-dense subsets exist for every $\varepsilon>0$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-finite-approximation-3eps
entries.title: Example 11.1.5 — correspondences bound $\dGH$ by $3\varepsilon$
entries.body:
Suppose $A=\{x_1,\dots,x_k\}\subset X$, $B=\{y_1,\dots,y_k\}\subset Y$ are
  $\varepsilon$-dense subsets with
  $\big||x_ix_j|-|y_iy_j|\big|\le\varepsilon$ for $1\le i,j\le k$. Then
  $\dGH(X,Y)\le3\varepsilon$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-lens-spaces-limit
entries.title: Example 11.1.6 — lens spaces collapse to $S^2(1/2)$
entries.body:
Let $M_k=S^3/\mathbb{Z}_k$ with the metric induced from $S^3(1)$. The
  Riemannian submersion $M_k\to S^2(1/2)$ has fibres of diameter
  $2\pi/k\to0$; using \cref{ex:pet-ch11-finite-approximation-3eps} it follows
  that $M_k\to S^2(1/2)$ in the Gromov--Hausdorff topology.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-berger-spheres-limit
entries.title: Example 11.1.7 — Berger spheres collapse to $S^2(1/2)$
entries.body:
Likewise the Berger metrics $(S^3,g_\varepsilon)\to S^2(1/2)$ as
  $\varepsilon\to0$. In both examples the volume goes to zero while the
  curvatures and diameters stay uniformly bounded; in the Berger case the
  manifolds are even simply connected, yet the topology changes drastically —
  in the lens-space case the fundamental groups of the sequence elements are
  even mutually different.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-gh-space-separable-complete
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.1.8 — $(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$ separable and complete
entries.body: The metric space $(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$ is separable and complete.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-pointed-gh-distance
entries.title: pointed Gromov--Hausdorff distance
entries.body:
For pointed metric spaces $(X,x)$, $(Y,y)$, the \emph{pointed
  Gromov--Hausdorff distance} is
  \[
    \dGH\big((X,x),(Y,y)\big)=\inf\{d_H(X,Y)+|xy|\},
  \]
  the infimum over admissible metrics on $X\cup Y$ of the Hausdorff distance
  plus the distance between the marked points.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-pointed-gh-topology
entries.title: pointed Gromov--Hausdorff topology
entries.body:
On the collection $\mathcal{M}_*=\{(X,x,|\cdot|)\}$ of proper pointed
  metric spaces, $(X_i,x_i,|\cdot|_i)\to(X,x,|\cdot|)$ in the \emph{pointed
  Gromov--Hausdorff topology} if for every $R$ there is a sequence $R_i\to R$
  such that the closed balls
  $\big(\overline{B}(x_i,R_i),x_i,|\cdot|_i\big)\to\big(\overline{B}(x,R),x,|\cdot|\big)$
  converge in the pointed Gromov--Hausdorff metric of
  \cref{def:pet-ch11-pointed-gh-distance}.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-convergence-of-maps
entries.title: convergence of maps
entries.body:
Given $f_k:X_k\to Y_k$ with $X_k\to X$ and $Y_k\to Y$ (Gromov--Hausdorff),
  $f_k$ \emph{converges} to $f:X\to Y$ if for every sequence $x_k\in X_k$
  with $x_k\to x\in X$ one has $f_k(x_k)\to f(x)$. This depends on the choice
  of Hausdorff-convergent ambient metrics. It is a strong notion: if
  $X_k=X$, $Y_k=Y$, $f_k=f$ for all $k$, then $f$ converges to itself only if
  $f$ is continuous.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-map-convergence-as-extension
entries.title: convergence of maps as an extension problem
entries.body:
Convergence of maps preserves being distance preserving or a submetry. A
  sequence $f_k:X_k\to Y_k$ can be regarded as one continuous map
  $F:\bigcup_iX_i\to Y\cup\bigcup_iY_i$; the sequence converges iff $F$ has a
  continuous extension $X\cup\bigcup_iX_i\to Y\cup\bigcup_iY_i$ restricting to
  the limit map on $X$. When the $X_i$ are compact this is equivalent to $F$
  being uniformly continuous.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-equicontinuous-family
entries.title: equicontinuous family
entries.body:
A family $f_k:X_k\to Y_k$ is \emph{equicontinuous} if for every
  $\varepsilon>0$ and $x_k\in X_k$ there is $\delta>0$ with
  $f_k(B(x_k,\delta))\subset B(f_k(x_k),\varepsilon)$ for all $k$. Uniformly
  Lipschitz families are equicontinuous.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-arzela-ascoli-gh
entries.title: Lemma 11.1.9 — Gromov--Hausdorff Arzelà--Ascoli
entries.body:
An equicontinuous family $f_k:X_k\to Y_k$, with $X_k\to X$ and $Y_k\to Y$
  in the (pointed) Gromov--Hausdorff topology, has a convergent subsequence.
  In the pointed case, one also assumes each $f_k$ preserves the base point.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-capacity-covering-functions
entries.title: capacity and covering functions
entries.body:
For a compact metric space $X$, let
  $\Cap(\varepsilon)=\Cap_X(\varepsilon)$ be the maximum number of disjoint
  $\varepsilon/2$-balls in $X$, and $\Cov(\varepsilon)=\Cov_X(\varepsilon)$
  the minimum number of $\varepsilon$-balls needed to cover $X$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-cov-le-cap
entries.title: covering is bounded by capacity
entries.body: $\Cov(\varepsilon)\le\Cap(\varepsilon)$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-gh-close-cov-cap-comparison
entries.title: covering/capacity under Gromov--Hausdorff proximity
entries.body:
If $\dGH(X,Y)<\delta$, then
  $\Cov_X(\varepsilon+2\delta)\le\Cov_Y(\varepsilon)$ and
  $\Cap_X(\varepsilon)\ge\Cap_Y(\varepsilon+2\delta)$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-gromov-precompactness-criterion
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.1.10 (Gromov, 1980) — precompactness criterion
entries.body:
For $\mathcal{C}\subset(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$ with all diameters $\le D<\infty$,
  the following are equivalent: (1) $\mathcal{C}$ is precompact; (2) there is
  $N_1:(0,\infty)\to(0,\infty)$ with $\Cap_X(\varepsilon)\le N_1(\varepsilon)$
  for all $X\in\mathcal{C}$; (3) there is $N_2:(0,\infty)\to(0,\infty)$ with
  $\Cov_X(\varepsilon)\le N_2(\varepsilon)$ for all $X\in\mathcal{C}$.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-pointed-precompactness-criterion
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.1.11 — pointed precompactness
entries.body:
$\mathcal{C}\subset\mathcal{M}_*$ is precompact iff for each $R>0$ the
  collection $\{\overline{B}(x,R)\mid(X,x)\in\mathcal{C}\}\subset(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$
  is precompact.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-metric-doubling-condition
entries.title: metric doubling condition
entries.body:
$X$ satisfies the \emph{metric doubling condition} with constant $C$ if
  every ball $B(p,R)$ can be covered by at most $C$ balls of radius $R/2$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-doubling-implies-precompact
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.1.12 — doubling implies precompact
entries.body:
If every $X\in\mathcal{C}\subset(\mathcal{M},\dGH)$ satisfies the metric
  doubling condition with constant $C<\infty$ and $\diam X\le D<\infty$, then
  $\mathcal{C}$ is precompact.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-ricci-diam-precompactness
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.1.13 — Ricci/diameter precompactness
entries.body:
For $n\ge2$, $k\in\mathbb{R}$, $D>0$: (1) the class of closed Riemannian
  $n$-manifolds with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$, $\diam\le D$ is precompact; (2) the
  class of pointed complete Riemannian $n$-manifolds with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$ is
  precompact.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-cm-norm
entries.title: $C^0$ and $C^m$ norms
entries.body:
For a bounded domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$, the bounded continuous
  functions $u:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}^k$ form $C^0(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ with sup-norm
  $\|u\|_{C^0}=\sup_{x\in\Omega}|u(x)|$, a Banach space. Using multi-index
  notation $\partial^Iu=\partial_1^{i_1}\cdots\partial_n^{i_n}u$ for
  $I=(i_1,\dots,i_n)$, $|I|=i_1+\dots+i_n$, define $C^m(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ as
  the functions with $m$ continuous partial derivatives, with norm
  \[
    \|u\|_{C^m}=\|u\|_{C^0}+\sum_{1\le|I|\le m}\|\partial^Iu\|_{C^0}.
  \]
  This makes $C^m(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ a Banach space, but the inclusions
  $C^m\subset C^{m-1}$ are not closed subspaces (e.g.\ $f(x)=|x|$ lies in the
  $C^0([-1,1],\mathbb{R})$-closure of $C^1([-1,1],\mathbb{R})$).

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-holder-pseudonorm
entries.title: Hölder pseudo-norm
entries.body:
For $\alpha\in(0,1]$ and $u:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}^k$, the \emph{$C^\alpha$
  pseudo-norm} is
  \[
    \|u\|_\alpha=\sup_{x,y\in\Omega}\frac{|u(x)-u(y)|}{|x-y|^\alpha}.
  \]
  When $\alpha=1$ this is the best Lipschitz constant for $u$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-holder-space
entries.title: Hölder space $C^{m,\alpha}$
entries.body:
The \emph{Hölder space} $C^{m,\alpha}(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ consists of the
  $u\in C^m(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ whose $m$th-order partial derivatives all have
  finite $C^\alpha$ pseudo-norm, with norm
  \[
    \|u\|_{C^{m,\alpha}}=\|u\|_{C^m}+\sum_{|I|=m}\|\partial^Iu\|_\alpha.
  \]
  If the domain needs to be specified, one writes $\|u\|_{C^{m,\alpha},\Omega}$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-holder-space-banach-compact-inclusion
entries.title: Lemma 11.2.1 — Hölder spaces are Banach, compactly nested
entries.body:
$C^{m,\alpha}(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ is a Banach space, and for $\beta<\alpha$ the
  inclusion $C^{m,\alpha}(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)\subset C^{m,\beta}(\Omega,\mathbb{R}^k)$ is
  always compact (maps closed bounded sets to compact sets).

deps.title: Lemma 11.2.1 — Hölder spaces are Banach, compactly nested
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-holder-space-banach-compact-inclusion

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-elliptic-operator
entries.title: elliptic operator
entries.body:
An operator $Lu=a^{ij}\partial_i\partial_ju+b^i\partial_iu$ ($a^{ij}=a^{ji}$)
  is \emph{elliptic} when $(a^{ij})$ is positive definite. Throughout, all
  eigenvalues of $(a^{ij})$ lie in $[\lambda,\lambda^{-1}]$, $\lambda>0$, and
  $\|a^{ij}\|_\alpha\le\lambda^{-1}$, $\|b^i\|_\alpha\le\lambda^{-1}$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-schauder-estimates
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.2.2 — Schauder estimates
entries.body:
Let $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ have diameter $\le D$ and $K\subset\Omega$ with
  $d(K,\partial\Omega)\ge\delta$. For $\alpha\in(0,1)$ there is
  $C=C(n,\alpha,\lambda,\delta,D)$ with
  \[
    \|u\|_{C^{2,\alpha},K}\le C\big(\|Lu\|_{C^0,\Omega}+\|u\|_{C^0,\Omega}\big),
    \qquad
    \|u\|_{C^{1,\alpha},K}\le C\big(\|Lu\|_{C^0,\Omega}+\|u\|_{C^0,\Omega}\big).
  \]
  If in addition $\Omega$ has smooth boundary and $u=\varphi$ on
  $\partial\Omega$, there is $C=C(n,\alpha,\lambda,D)$ with
  \[
    \|u\|_{C^{2,\alpha},\Omega}\le C\big(\|Lu\|_{C^0,\Omega}+\|\varphi\|_{C^{2,\alpha},\partial\Omega}\big).
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-divergence-form-laplace-beltrami
entries.title: divergence form and the Laplace--Beltrami operator
entries.body:
One proves \cref{thm:pet-ch11-schauder-estimates} first for $Lu=\Delta u=\delta^{ij}\partial_i\partial_ju$,
  then, via a linear change of coordinates, for constant-coefficient
  operators $Lu=a^{ij}\partial_i\partial_ju$; Schauder's trick is that the
  hypotheses on $a^{ij}$ make it locally almost constant, and a partition of
  unity finishes the argument. The first-order term causes no extra trouble,
  especially when $L$ is in \emph{divergence form}
  $Lu=a^{ij}\partial_i\partial_ju+b^i\partial_iu=\partial_i(a^{ij}\partial_ju)$,
  which integrates by parts as
  $\int_\Omega\partial_i(a^{ij}\partial_ju)\,h=-\int_\Omega a^{ij}\partial_iu\,\partial_ih$
  for $h=0$ on $\partial\Omega$. The Laplacian of a Riemannian metric $g$ is of
  this form in local coordinates:
  \[
    Lu=\Delta_gu=\frac1{\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}}\partial_i\big(\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}\,g^{ij}\partial_ju\big),
  \]
  so $\int v\,Lu\,\vol=\int v\,\partial_i\big(\sqrt{\det g_{ij}}\,g^{ij}\partial_ju\big)$.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-higher-schauder-estimates
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.2.3 — higher-order Schauder estimates
entries.body:
If also $\|a^{ij}\|_{C^{m,\alpha}},\|b^i\|_{C^{m,\alpha}}\le\lambda^{-1}$,
  there is $C=C(n,m,\alpha,\lambda,\delta,D)$ with
  \[
    \|u\|_{C^{m+2,\alpha},K}\le C\big(\|Lu\|_{C^{m,\alpha},\Omega}+\|u\|_{C^0,\Omega}\big),
  \]
  and on a domain with smooth boundary,
  $\|u\|_{C^{m+2,\alpha},\Omega}\le C\big(\|Lu\|_{C^{m,\alpha},\Omega}+\|\varphi\|_{C^{m+2,\alpha},\partial\Omega}\big)$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-dirichlet-problem-unique-solution
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.2.4 — Dirichlet problem
entries.body:
If $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ is a bounded domain with smooth boundary, the
  Dirichlet problem $Lu=f$, $u|_{\partial\Omega}=\varphi$ always has a unique
  solution $u\in C^{2,\alpha}(\Omega)$ when $f\in C^\alpha(\Omega)$,
  $\varphi\in C^{2,\alpha}(\partial\Omega)$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-harmonic-coordinates-exist
entries.title: Lemma 11.2.5 — existence of harmonic coordinates
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is an $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifold and $p\in M$, there is
  a neighborhood $U\ni p$ carrying a \emph{harmonic coordinate system}
  $x=(x^1,\dots,x^n):U\to\mathbb{R}^n$, i.e.\ each $x^i$ is harmonic for
  $\Delta_g$.

deps.title: divergence form and the Laplace--Beltrami operator
deps.label: rem:pet-ch11-divergence-form-laplace-beltrami

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords
entries.title: Lemma 11.2.6 — Laplacian and Ricci curvature in harmonic coordinates

deps.title: Lemma 11.2.6 — Laplacian and Ricci curvature in harmonic coordinates
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-einstein-harmonic-regularity-bootstrap
entries.title: Einstein metrics in harmonic coordinates are smooth
entries.body:
For an Einstein metric $\Ric_{ij}=(n-1)kg_{ij}$,
  \cref{lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords} reads
  $\tfrac12\Delta g_{ij}=-(n-1)kg_{ij}-Q(g,\partial g)$, which makes sense
  once $g_{ij}\in C^1$ and, after multiplying by a test function and
  integrating by parts, can be interpreted weakly. If $g_{ij}\in C^{1,\alpha}$
  the right side lies in some $C^\beta$, so \cref{cor:pet-ch11-higher-schauder-estimates}
  gives $g_{ij}\in C^{2,\beta}$; bootstrapping shows $g_{ij}\in C^\infty$
  (in fact real-analytic). Hence any metric that is, in harmonic coordinates,
  a weak solution of the Einstein equation is automatically smooth.

deps.title: Lemma 11.2.6 — Laplacian and Ricci curvature in harmonic coordinates
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-cmalpha-norm-pointed
entries.title: $C^{m,\alpha}$ norm at scale $r$
entries.body:
For a pointed Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M,g,p)$, say
  $\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$ if there is a $C^{m+1,\alpha}$ chart
  $\varphi:(B(0,r),0)\subset\mathbb{R}^n\to(U,p)\subset M$ such that
  \begin{align}
    \text{(n1)}\quad&|D\varphi|\le e^Q\text{ on }B(0,r),\ |D\varphi^{-1}|\le e^Q\text{ on }U,
    \text{ equivalently }e^{-2Q}\delta_{kl}v^kv^l\le g_{kl}v^kv^l\le e^{2Q}\delta_{kl}v^kv^l\ \forall v\in\mathbb{R}^n,\\
    \text{(n2)}\quad&r^{|I|+\alpha}\|\partial^Ig_{ij}\|_\alpha\le Q\text{ for all multi-indices }I,\ 0\le|I|\le m.
  \end{align}

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-cmalpha-norm-global
entries.title: global $C^{m,\alpha}$ norm of a manifold
entries.body:
$\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=\sup_{p\in M}\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$. The
  charts are regarded as maps from the fixed model $B(0,r)$ into $M$, so that
  the norm measures how far the manifold differs from a Euclidean ball: (n1)
  bounds the metric coefficients from above and below in the chosen
  coordinates (giving uniform ellipticity for the Laplacian), and (n2)
  bounds the derivatives of the metric. The definition makes sense for
  merely $C^{m,\alpha}$ metrics, without assuming more smoothness; some
  constructions (e.g.\ exponential maps) may then be ill-defined if $m\le1$.
  The pointed norm is always finite, but the global norm need not be finite
  on any scale when $M$ is noncompact.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-flat-manifold-norm-zero
entries.title: Example 11.3.1 — flat manifolds have norm zero
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is a complete flat manifold then $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=0$
  for all $r\le\inj(M,g)$; in particular
  $\|(\mathbb{R}^n,g_{\mathrm{eu}})\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=0$ for all $r$.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-tensor-convergence-compact-subset
entries.title: $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence of tensors on a compact subset
entries.body:
Fix a closed manifold $M$, or a precompact subset $A\subset M$. A sequence
  of functions on $A$ \emph{converges in $C^{m,\alpha}$} if it converges in
  the charts of some fixed finite covering of coordinate patches that are
  uniformly bi-Lipschitz; this is independent of the covering. A sequence of
  tensors converges in $C^{m,\alpha}$ if its components converge in these
  patches, giving a notion of convergence of Riemannian metrics on compact
  subsets of a fixed manifold.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-pointed-cmalpha-convergence
entries.title: pointed $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence of manifolds
entries.body:
A sequence of pointed complete Riemannian manifolds converges in the
  \emph{pointed $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology}, $(M_i,g_i,p_i)\to(M,g,p)$, if for
  every $R>0$ there is a domain $\Omega\supset B(p,R)\subset M$ and
  embeddings $F_i:\Omega\to M_i$ for large $i$ with $F_i(p)=p_i$,
  $F_i(\Omega)\supset B(p_i,R)$, and $F_i^*g_i\to g$ on $\Omega$ in the
  $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology.

deps.title: $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence of tensors on a compact subset
deps.label: def:pet-ch11-tensor-convergence-compact-subset

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-cmalpha-convergence-implies-gh
entries.title: pointed $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence implies GH convergence
entries.body:
Pointed $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence implies pointed Gromov--Hausdorff
  convergence. When all manifolds are closed with uniformly bounded
  diameter, the $F_i$ are diffeomorphisms, so one may speak of unpointed
  convergence, which forces all manifolds in the tail of the sequence to be
  diffeomorphic; classes of closed manifolds precompact in some $C^{m,\alpha}$
  topology contain at most finitely many diffeomorphism types.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-cmalpha-convergence-pullback-warning
entries.title: pullback convergence versus classical convergence
entries.body:
Metrics $g_i=F_i^*g$ for diffeomorphisms $F_i$ of a fixed $M$ can converge
  in the pointed $C^{m,\alpha}$ sense of
  \cref{def:pet-ch11-pointed-cmalpha-convergence} without converging in any
  fixed coordinate system: e.g., on $M=S^2$ with the standard metric $g$, let
  $F_i$ be diffeomorphisms from the flow of a vector field vanishing only at
  the poles and pointing toward the south pole. Away from the poles,
  $F_i^*g\to0$ in fixed coordinates as the flow collapses the sphere toward
  the south pole, so $(S^2,F_i^*g)$ does not converge classically; but
  $(S^2,F_i^*g)$ is isometric to $(S^2,g)$ for every $i$, so as pointed
  Riemannian manifolds up to isometry the sequence is constant.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
entries.body:
Given $(M,g,p)$, $m\ge0$, $\alpha\in(0,1]$:
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item[(1)] $\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=\|(M,\lambda^2g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},\lambda r}$ for all $\lambda>0$.
  \item[(2)] $r\mapsto\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ is increasing, continuous, and $\to0$ as $r\to0$.
  \item[(3)] If $(M_i,g_i,p_i)\to(M,g,p)$ in $C^{m,\alpha}$, then
    $\|(M_i,g_i,p_i)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\to\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ for all $r>0$;
    if in addition all manifolds have uniformly bounded diameter,
    $\|(M_i,g_i)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\to\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ for all $r>0$.
  \item[(4)] If $\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}<Q$, then for $x_1,x_2\in B(0,r)$,
    $e^{-Q}\min\{|x_1-x_2|,2r-|x_1|-|x_2|\}\le|\varphi(x_1)\varphi(x_2)|\le e^Q|x_1-x_2|$.
  \item[(5)] The infimum $Q=\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ is attained by some
    chart satisfying (n1), (n2) with this $Q$.
  \item[(6)] When $M$ is compact, the supremum $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=\sup_p\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ is a maximum.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
deps.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-norm-bound-ball-containment
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.3.3 — ball containment in the chart domain
entries.body: If $\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$, then $B(p,e^{-Q}r)\subset U$.

deps.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
deps.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.3.3 — ball containment in the chart domain
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-norm-bound-ball-containment

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-norm-zero-implies-flat
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.3.4 — norm zero implies flat
entries.body:
If $\|(M,g,p)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}=0$ for some $r$, then $p$ has a flat
  neighborhood.

deps.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
deps.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.3.3 — ball containment in the chart domain
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-norm-bound-ball-containment

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-harmonic-norm
entries.title: harmonic norm
entries.body:
The \emph{harmonic norm} $\|(M,g,p)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$ is
  defined exactly as in \cref{def:pet-ch11-cmalpha-norm-pointed}, except that
  $\varphi^{-1}:U\to\mathbb{R}^n$ is additionally required to be harmonic for
  $g$, i.e.\ for each $j$,
  $\frac1{\sqrt{\det[g_{ij}]}}\partial_j\big(\sqrt{\det[g_{ij}]}\,g^{ij}\big)=0$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch11-harmonic-norm-properties
entries.title: Prop.\ 11.3.5 (Anderson, 1990) — harmonic-norm analogue
entries.body:
\cref{prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties} holds for the harmonic norm when
  $m\ge1$.

deps.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
deps.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-precompact-gh
entries.title: Fundamental Theorem, part A — Gromov--Hausdorff precompactness
entries.body:
For $Q>0$, $n\ge2$, $m\ge0$, $\alpha\in(0,1]$, $r>0$, let
  $\mathcal{M}=\mathcal{M}^{m,\alpha}(n,Q,r)$ be the class of complete,
  pointed Riemannian $n$-manifolds $(M,g,p)$ with
  $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$. Then $\mathcal{M}$ is precompact in the
  pointed Gromov--Hausdorff topology.

deps.title: Fundamental Theorem, part A — Gromov--Hausdorff precompactness
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-precompact-gh

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-limit-is-manifold
entries.title: Fundamental Theorem, part B — the limit is a norm-bounded manifold
entries.body:
In the setting of \cref{lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-precompact-gh}, if
  $(M_i,g_i,p_i)\in\mathcal{M}$ converge to $(X,|\cdot|,p)$ in the pointed
  Gromov--Hausdorff topology, then $X$ carries the structure of a $C^{m,\alpha}$
  Riemannian manifold with $\|(X,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$, whose induced
  distance agrees with $|\cdot|$.

deps.title: Fundamental Theorem, part A — Gromov--Hausdorff precompactness
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-precompact-gh

deps.title: Prop.\ 11.3.2 — elementary properties of the norm
deps.label: prop:pet-ch11-norm-properties

deps.title: Fundamental Theorem, part B — the limit is a norm-bounded manifold
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-limit-is-manifold

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
entries.body:
For $Q>0$, $n\ge2$, $m\ge0$, $\alpha\in(0,1]$, $r>0$, the class
  $\mathcal{M}^{m,\alpha}(n,Q,r)$ of complete pointed Riemannian $n$-manifolds
  $(M,g,p)$ with $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$ is compact in the pointed
  $C^{m,\beta}$ topology for all $\beta<\alpha$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

deps.title: Fundamental Theorem, part A — Gromov--Hausdorff precompactness
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-precompact-gh

deps.title: Fundamental Theorem, part B — the limit is a norm-bounded manifold
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-limit-is-manifold

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-fundamental-thm-diam-vol-bounded
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.3.7 — diameter/volume bounds give finiteness
entries.body:
Subclasses of $\mathcal{M}^{m,\alpha}(n,Q,r)$ whose elements additionally
  satisfy $\diam\le D$, respectively $\vol\le V$, are compact in the
  $C^{m,\beta}$ topology (for $\beta<\alpha$); in particular each such
  subclass contains only finitely many diffeomorphism types.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-harmonic-norm-class-compact
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.3.8 — compactness for the harmonic norm
entries.body:
For $Q>0$, $n\ge2$, $m\ge0$, $\alpha\in(0,1]$, $r>0$, the class of complete
  pointed Riemannian $n$-manifolds $(M,g,p)$ with
  $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$ is closed in the pointed
  $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology and compact in the pointed $C^{m,\beta}$ topology
  for all $\beta<\alpha$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.3.8 — compactness for the harmonic norm
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-harmonic-norm-class-compact

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-alternative-norm-conditions
entries.title: alternative norm conditions
entries.body:
The norm concept and its properties are unchanged if (n1), (n2) are
  replaced by
  \[
    \text{(n1$'$)}\quad|D\varphi|,|D\varphi^{-1}|\le f_1(n,Q),\qquad
    \text{(n2$'$)}\quad r^{|I|+\alpha}\|\partial^Ig\|_\alpha\le f_2(n,Q),\ 0\le|I|\le m,
  \]
  for continuous $f_1,f_2$ with $f_1(n,0)=1$, $f_2(n,0)=0$: this preserves
  continuity of the norm in $r$, the Fundamental Theorem, and the
  characterization of flat manifolds and Euclidean space.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-c0-norm-precompactness-only
entries.title: the $C^0$ norm and topological finiteness
entries.body:
Taking $m=\alpha=0$, (n2) no longer makes sense but a $C^0$-norm concept
  survives. The class $\mathcal{M}^0(n,Q,r)$ is only precompact in the
  pointed Gromov--Hausdorff topology (the flat-manifold characterization
  still holds), and diameter- or volume-bounded subclasses are only
  Gromov--Hausdorff precompact, with diffeomorphism-type finiteness
  apparently failing: the averaging construction of
  \cref{thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence} breaks down since the
  coordinates converge only in $C^{\alpha}$, $\alpha<1$. Using local
  contractibility of homeomorphism groups, two $C^0$-close topological
  embeddings can nonetheless be glued without much $C^0$ distortion, giving
  topological embeddings with converging pullback metrics; hence
  diameter- or volume-bounded classes contain only finitely many
  homeomorphism types, matching the original form of Cheeger's finiteness
  theorem (which considered diffeomorphism types under stronger coordinate
  bounds).

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-reifenberg-norm
entries.title: Reifenberg norm
entries.body:
For a metric space $(X,|\cdot|)$, the \emph{$n$-dimensional Reifenberg norm
  at scale $r$} is
  \[
    \|(X,|\cdot|)\|^n_r=\frac1r\sup_{p\in X}\dGH\big(B(p,r),B(0,r)\big),
  \]
  where $B(0,R)\subset\mathbb{R}^n$; the factor $r^{-1}$ prevents small distances
  merely because $r$ is small. If $(X_i,|\cdot|_i)\to(X,|\cdot|)$ in the
  Gromov--Hausdorff topology then $\|(X_i,|\cdot|_i)\|^n_r\to\|(X,|\cdot|)\|^n_r$
  for fixed $n,r$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-reifenberg-norm-properties
entries.title: Reifenberg norm: vanishing and the Cheeger--Colding converse
entries.body:
For an $n$-dimensional Riemannian manifold,
  $\lim_{r\to0}\|(M,g)\|^n_r=0$. Cheeger and Colding proved a converse: there
  is $\varepsilon(n)>0$ such that if $\|(X,|\cdot|)\|^n_r\le\varepsilon(n)$
  for all small $r$, then $X$ is, in a weak sense, an $n$-dimensional
  Riemannian manifold; in particular, for small $r$, the $\alpha$-Hölder
  distance between $B(p,r)$ and $B(0,r)$ is then small.

entries.label: def:pet-ch11-holder-distance
entries.title: $\alpha$-Hölder distance
entries.body:
The \emph{$\alpha$-Hölder distance} $d_\alpha(X,Y)$ between metric spaces is
  the infimum, over homeomorphisms $F:X\to Y$, of
  \[
    \log\max\Big\{\sup_{x_1\ne x_2}\frac{|F(x_1)F(x_2)|}{|x_1x_2|^\alpha},\
    \sup_{y_1\ne y_2}\frac{|F^{-1}(y_1)F^{-1}(y_2)|}{|y_1y_2|^\alpha}\Big\}.
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-cheeger-colding-holder-convergence
entries.title: Cheeger--Colding Hölder convergence and Colding's volume criterion
entries.body:
Cheeger and Colding show that if $(M_i,g_i)\to(X,|\cdot|)$ in the
  Gromov--Hausdorff topology with $\|(M_i,g_i)\|^n_r\le\varepsilon(n)$ for all
  $i$ and small $r$, then $(M_i,g_i)\to(X,|\cdot|)$ in the Hölder distance,
  and in particular all $M_i$ are diffeomorphic to $X$ for large $i$.
  Colding earlier showed that for $(M,g)$ with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$,
  $\|(M,g)\|^n_r$ is small iff $\vol B(p,r)\ge(1-\delta)\vol B(0,r)$ for some
  small $\delta$; relative volume comparison shows the volume condition for
  one $r$ persists for all smaller $r$, so smallness of the Reifenberg norm
  for all small $r$ follows from the volume condition at a single $r$.

deps.title: Reifenberg norm: vanishing and the Cheeger--Colding converse
deps.label: rem:pet-ch11-reifenberg-norm-properties

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-ricci-harmonic-c1alpha-bound
entries.title: Lemma 11.4.1 (Anderson, 1990) — Ricci controls the harmonic $C^{1,\alpha}$ norm
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ has $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$. For $r_1<r_2$,
  $K\ge\|(M,g,p)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{1,r_2}}$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$ there is
  $C(n,\alpha,K,r_1,r_2,\Lambda)$ with
  $\|(M,g,p)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{1,\alpha},r_1}\le C$. If moreover $g$ is
  Einstein, $\Ric=kg$, then for each $m$ there is
  $C(n,\alpha,K,r_1,r_2,k,m)$ with
  $\|(M,g,p)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{m+1,\alpha},r_1}\le C$.

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.1 (Anderson, 1990) — Ricci controls the harmonic $C^{1,\alpha}$ norm
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-ricci-harmonic-c1alpha-bound

deps.title: Lemma 11.2.6 — Laplacian and Ricci curvature in harmonic coordinates
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-ricci-bounded-precompact-c1alpha
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.2 — precompactness under bounded Ricci and harmonic norm
entries.body:
For $n\ge2$, $Q,r,\Lambda\in(0,\infty)$, the class of Riemannian
  $n$-manifolds with $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{1,r}}\le Q$,
  $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$ is precompact in the pointed $C^{1,\alpha}$ topology for
  every $\alpha\in(0,1)$; the subclass of Einstein manifolds is compact in
  the $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology for every $m\ge1$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.1 (Anderson, 1990) — Ricci controls the harmonic $C^{1,\alpha}$ norm
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-ricci-harmonic-c1alpha-bound

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-anderson-injectivity-radius-controls-norm
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.3 (Anderson, 1990) — injectivity radius controls the harmonic norm
entries.body:
Given $n\ge2$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$, $\Lambda,R>0$, for each $Q>0$ there is
  $r(n,\alpha,\Lambda,R)>0$ such that any compact Riemannian $n$-manifold
  $(M,g)$ with $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$, $\inj\ge R$ satisfies
  $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{1,\alpha},r}\le Q$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.3 (Anderson, 1990) — injectivity radius controls the harmonic norm
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-anderson-injectivity-radius-controls-norm

deps.title: Einstein metrics in harmonic coordinates are smooth
deps.label: rem:pet-ch11-einstein-harmonic-regularity-bootstrap

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.1 (Anderson, 1990) — Ricci controls the harmonic $C^{1,\alpha}$ norm
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-ricci-harmonic-c1alpha-bound

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-anderson-diffeo-finiteness-injectivity
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.4 (Anderson, 1990) — diffeomorphism finiteness under Ricci, diameter, injectivity bounds
entries.body:
For $n\ge2$, $\Lambda,D,R>0$, the class of closed Riemannian $n$-manifolds
  with $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$, $\diam\le D$, $\inj\ge R$ is precompact in the
  $C^{1,\alpha}$ topology for every $\alpha\in(0,1)$, and in particular
  contains only finitely many diffeomorphism types.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.3 (Anderson, 1990) — injectivity radius controls the harmonic norm
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-anderson-injectivity-radius-controls-norm

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-volume-pinching-euclidean
entries.title: Lemma 11.4.5 (Anderson, 1990) — near-maximal volume growth forces Euclidean space
entries.body:
For each $n\ge2$ there is $\varepsilon(n)>0$ such that any complete Ricci
  flat $(M,g)$ with $\vol B(p,r)\ge(1-\varepsilon)\omega_nr^n$ for some
  $p\in M$ and some $r$ is isometric to Euclidean space.

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.5 (Anderson, 1990) — near-maximal volume growth forces Euclidean space
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-volume-pinching-euclidean

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-volume-pinching-precompactness
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.6 — volume-pinching precompactness
entries.body:
For $n\ge2$, $-\infty<\lambda\le\Lambda<\infty$, $D,R\in(0,\infty)$, there
  is $\delta=\delta(n,\lambda R^2)$ such that the class of closed Riemannian
  $n$-manifolds with $(n-1)\Lambda\ge\Ric\ge(n-1)\lambda$, $\diam\le D$,
  $\vol B(p,R)\ge(1-\delta)v(n,\lambda,R)$ is precompact in the $C^{1,\alpha}$
  topology for every $\alpha\in(0,1)$, and in particular contains only
  finitely many diffeomorphism types.

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.5 (Anderson, 1990) — near-maximal volume growth forces Euclidean space
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-anderson-volume-pinching-euclidean

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-convergence-theorem-riemannian-geometry
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.7 — the Convergence Theorem of Riemannian Geometry
entries.body:
Given $R,K>0$ there exist $Q,r>0$ such that any $(M,g)$ with $\inj\ge R$,
  $|\sec|\le K$ has $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{1,\alpha},r}\le Q$; in
  particular this class is compact in the pointed $C^{1,\alpha}$ topology for
  all $\alpha<1$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.3 (Anderson, 1990) — injectivity radius controls the harmonic norm
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-anderson-injectivity-radius-controls-norm

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-cheeger-even-dim-finiteness
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.8 (Cheeger, 1967) — even-dimensional pinched curvature finiteness
entries.body:
For $n\ge1$, $k>0$, the class of Riemannian $2n$-manifolds with
  $k\le\sec\le1$ is compact in the $C^{1,\alpha}$ topology and hence contains
  only finitely many diffeomorphism types (using the diameter bound in
  positive curvature and Klingenberg's injectivity-radius estimate).

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.7 — the Convergence Theorem of Riemannian Geometry
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-convergence-theorem-riemannian-geometry

entries.label: lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma
entries.title: Lemma 11.4.9 (Cheeger, 1967) — volume lower bound controls injectivity radius
entries.body:
Given $n\ge2$, $v,K>0$, and a compact $n$-manifold $(M,g)$ with
  $|\sec|\le K$ and $\vol B(p,1)\ge v$ for all $p\in M$, then $\inj M\ge R$
  for $R=R(n,K,v)$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.9 (Cheeger, 1967) — volume lower bound controls injectivity radius
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-cheeger-diam-vol-finiteness
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.10 (Cheeger, 1967) — bounded curvature, diameter, volume finiteness
entries.body:
For $n\ge2$, $K,D,v>0$, the class of closed Riemannian $n$-manifolds with
  $|\sec|\le K$, $\diam\le D$, $\vol\ge v$ is precompact in the $C^{1,\alpha}$
  topology for every $\alpha\in(0,1)$, and in particular contains only
  finitely many diffeomorphism types.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.7 — the Convergence Theorem of Riemannian Geometry
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-convergence-theorem-riemannian-geometry

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.9 (Cheeger, 1967) — volume lower bound controls injectivity radius
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-lower-sec-bound-injectivity-norm-control
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.11 — lower sectional curvature and injectivity radius control the norm
entries.body:
Given $R,k>0$ there exist $Q,r$ depending on $R,k$ such that any $(M,g)$
  with $\sec\ge-k^2$, $\inj\ge R$ satisfies $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{1,r}}\le Q$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.11 — lower sectional curvature and injectivity radius control the norm
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-lower-sec-bound-injectivity-norm-control

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-optimality-lower-curvature-example
entries.title: Example 11.4.12 — optimality: only $C^{1,1}$ limits under lower curvature
entries.body:
For $\varepsilon\to0$, rotationally symmetric metrics
  $dr^2+\phi_\varepsilon^2(r)d\theta^2$ with $\phi_\varepsilon$ concave,
  $\phi_\varepsilon(r)=r$ for $0\le r\le1-\varepsilon$ and
  $\phi_\varepsilon(r)=\tfrac34r$ for $r\ge1+\varepsilon$, have $\sec\ge0$,
  $\inj\ge1$, and converge to a $C^{1,1}$ manifold with $C^{0,1}$ metric
  $(M,g)$, for which $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{1,r}}<\infty$ for all $r$: limit spaces
  of sequences with $\inj\ge R$, $\sec\ge-k^2$ cannot in general be smoother
  than this.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.11 — lower sectional curvature and injectivity radius control the norm
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-lower-sec-bound-injectivity-norm-control

entries.label: ex:pet-ch11-c11-limit-example
entries.title: Example 11.4.13 — a $C^{1,1}$ limit with two-sided curvature bound
entries.body:
With $\psi_\varepsilon(r)=\sin r$ for $0\le r\le\pi/2-\varepsilon$ and
  $\psi_\varepsilon(r)=1$ for $r\ge\pi/2$, the metrics
  $dr^2+\psi_\varepsilon^2(r)d\theta^2$ satisfy $|\sec|\le4$, $\inj\ge1/4$; as
  $\varepsilon\to0$ they converge to a $C^{1,1}$ limit metric, though only
  $C^{1,\alpha}$ convergence for $\alpha<1$ has been established for such
  limits. Unlike the two-sided case, no injectivity-radius lower bound need
  hold under a one-sided curvature bound alone.

deps.title: Example 11.4.12 — optimality: only $C^{1,1}$ limits under lower curvature
deps.label: ex:pet-ch11-optimality-lower-curvature-example

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-4-14
entries.title: Exercise 11.4.14
entries.body:
Given $a\in(0,1)$, $\varepsilon>0$, there is a smooth concave
  $\rho_\varepsilon(r)$ with $\rho_\varepsilon(r)=r$ for $0\le r\le\varepsilon$
  and $\rho_\varepsilon(r)=ar$ for $r\ge2\varepsilon$. The surfaces
  $dr^2+\rho_\varepsilon^2(r)d\theta^2$ have $\sec\ge0$, $\inj\le5\varepsilon$,
  and the volume of any $R$-ball is always $\ge a\pi R^2$.

deps.title: Example 11.4.12 — optimality: only $C^{1,1}$ limits under lower curvature
deps.label: ex:pet-ch11-optimality-lower-curvature-example

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-anderson-cheeger-ricci-lower-bound-norm
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.15 (Anderson--Cheeger, 1992) — Ricci lower bound with injectivity control
entries.body:
Given $R,k>0$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$, there exist $Q,r$ depending on $n,R,k$
  such that any $(M^n,g)$ with $\Ric\ge-(n-1)k^2$, $\inj\ge R$ satisfies
  $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{har}}_{C^{\alpha},r}\le Q$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-einstein-closeness
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.16 — Ricci pinching implies $C^{1,\alpha}$-closeness to Einstein
entries.body:
Given $n\ge2$, $R,D>0$, $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$, there is
  $\varepsilon(n,\lambda,D,R)>0$ such that any closed Riemannian $n$-manifold
  $(M,g)$ with $\diam\le D$, $\inj\ge R$, $|\Ric-\lambda g|\le\varepsilon$ is
  $C^{1,\alpha}$ close to an Einstein metric with Einstein constant $\lambda$.

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.4.4 (Anderson, 1990) — diffeomorphism finiteness under Ricci, diameter, injectivity bounds
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-anderson-diffeo-finiteness-injectivity

deps.title: Einstein metrics in harmonic coordinates are smooth
deps.label: rem:pet-ch11-einstein-harmonic-regularity-bootstrap

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.16 — Ricci pinching implies $C^{1,\alpha}$-closeness to Einstein
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-einstein-closeness

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-volume-alternative
entries.title: injectivity radius may be replaced by almost-maximal volume
entries.body:
Using \cref{cor:pet-ch11-volume-pinching-precompactness} in place of the
  injectivity-radius precompactness in the proof of
  \cref{thm:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-einstein-closeness}, the injectivity
  radius hypothesis there may be replaced by an almost-maximal-volume
  hypothesis.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.16 — Ricci pinching implies $C^{1,\alpha}$-closeness to Einstein
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-einstein-closeness

entries.label: thm:pet-ch11-sec-pinching-constant-curvature-closeness
entries.title: Thm.\ 11.4.17 — sectional-curvature pinching implies closeness to constant curvature
entries.body:
Given $n\ge2$, $v,D>0$, $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$, there is
  $\varepsilon(n,\lambda,D,v)>0$ such that any closed Riemannian $n$-manifold
  $(M,g)$ with $\diam\le D$, $\vol\ge v$, $|\sec-\lambda|\le\varepsilon$ is
  $C^{1,\alpha}$ close to a metric of constant curvature $\lambda$.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.17 — sectional-curvature pinching implies closeness to constant curvature
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-sec-pinching-constant-curvature-closeness

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.9 (Cheeger, 1967) — volume lower bound controls injectivity radius
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.16 — Ricci pinching implies $C^{1,\alpha}$-closeness to Einstein
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-ricci-pinching-einstein-closeness

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-even-dim-positive-pinching
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.18 — even-dimensional positive pinching without hypotheses
entries.body:
Given $2n\ge2$, $\lambda>0$, there is $\varepsilon=\varepsilon(n,\lambda)>0$
  such that any closed Riemannian $2n$-manifold $(M,g)$ with
  $|\sec-\lambda|\le\varepsilon$ is $C^{1,\alpha}$ close to a metric of
  constant curvature $\lambda$ (in even dimensions, positive curvature alone
  bounds the diameter and, together with an upper curvature bound, the
  injectivity radius from below).

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.4.17 — sectional-curvature pinching implies closeness to constant curvature
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-sec-pinching-constant-curvature-closeness

entries.label: cor:pet-ch11-odd-dim-simply-connected-pinching
entries.title: Cor.\ 11.4.19 — simply connected positive pinching
entries.body:
Given $n\ge2$, $\lambda>0$, there is $\varepsilon=\varepsilon(n,\lambda)>0$
  such that any closed simply connected Riemannian $n$-manifold $(M,g)$ with
  $|\sec-\lambda|\le\varepsilon$ is $C^{1,\alpha}$ close to a metric of
  constant curvature $\lambda$.

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.4.18 — even-dimensional positive pinching without hypotheses
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-even-dim-positive-pinching

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-1
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.1
entries.body:
Find a sequence of $1$-dimensional metric spaces that Hausdorff converge to
  the unit cube $[0,1]^3$ with the metric from the maximum norm on
  $\mathbb{R}^3$. Then find surfaces (jungle gyms) converging to the same
  space.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-2
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.2
entries.body:
If $F:X\to Y$ (not necessarily continuous) satisfies
  $\big||x_1x_2|-|F(x_1)F(x_2)|\big|\le\varepsilon$ for all $x_1,x_2\in X$ and
  $F(X)\subset Y$ is $\varepsilon$-dense, show $\dGH(X,Y)<2\varepsilon$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-3
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.3
entries.body:
(Croke) There is a universal constant $c(n)$ such that any $n$-manifold
  with $\inj\ge R$ satisfies $\vol B(p,r)\ge c(n)r^n$ for $r\le R/e$; use this
  to show the class of $n$-manifolds with $\inj\ge R$, $\vol\le V$ is
  precompact in the Gromov--Hausdorff topology.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-4
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.4
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete with $\Ric\ge(n-1)k$. Show there is
  $C(n,k)$ such that for each $\varepsilon\in(0,1)$ there is a cover by balls
  $B(x_i,\varepsilon)$ such that no more than $C(n,k)$ of the balls
  $B(x_i,5\varepsilon)$ have nonempty pairwise intersection.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-5
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.5
entries.body:
Show there are Bochner formulas for $\Hess(\tfrac12g(X,Y))$ and
  $\Delta g(X,Y)$, for vector fields $X,Y$ with symmetric $\nabla X,\nabla Y$,
  and use these to prove the formulas of
  \cref{lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords} relating Ricci
  curvature to the metric in harmonic coordinates.

deps.title: Lemma 11.2.6 — Laplacian and Ricci curvature in harmonic coordinates
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-laplacian-ricci-harmonic-coords

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-6
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.6
entries.body:
Show that, in contrast to the elliptic estimates of
  \cref{thm:pet-ch11-schauder-estimates}, it is not possible to find
  $C^{1,\gamma}$ bounds for a vector field $X$ in terms of $C^0$ bounds on
  $X$ and $\operatorname{div}X$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-7
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.7
entries.body:
Define $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence for incomplete manifolds, with the
  boundary $\partial$ the points in the completion but not the manifold.
  Show that the class of incomplete spaces with $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$ and
  $\inj(p)\ge\min\{R,R\cdot d(p,\partial)\}$, $R<1$, is precompact in the
  $C^{1,\alpha}$ topology.

deps.title: Cor.\ 11.4.4 (Anderson, 1990) — diffeomorphism finiteness under Ricci, diameter, injectivity bounds
deps.label: cor:pet-ch11-anderson-diffeo-finiteness-injectivity

deps.title: $C^{m,\alpha}$ convergence of tensors on a compact subset
deps.label: def:pet-ch11-tensor-convergence-compact-subset

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-8
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.8
entries.body:
Define a \emph{weighted norm}: fix $\rho(R)>0$ and require that, in a
  pointed manifold $(M,g,p)$, the points of $S(p,R)$ have norm $\le\rho(R)$.
  Prove the corresponding fundamental theorem.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-9
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.9
entries.body:
If $\mathcal{M}$ is a class of compact Riemannian $n$-manifolds compact in
  the $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology, show there is $f(r)\to0$ as $r\to0$, depending
  on $\mathcal{M}$, with $\|(M,g)\|_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le f(r)$ for all
  $M\in\mathcal{M}$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-10
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.10
entries.body:
The \emph{local models} of a class of Riemannian manifolds are the limits
  obtained by rescaling elements of the class by constants $\to\infty$; e.g.\
  $|\sec|\le K$ rescales to $|\sec|\le\lambda^{-2}K\to0$, so the local models
  are the flat manifolds, and adding $\inj\ge R$ forces $\inj\to\infty$ under
  rescaling, so the only local model is Euclidean space. Show that a class of
  spaces is compact in the $C^{m,\alpha}$ topology if every sequence in the
  class, rescaled by constants $\to\infty$, subconverges in $C^{m,\alpha}$ to
  Euclidean space.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-11
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.11
entries.body:
For the singular metric $dt^2+(at)^2d\theta^2$, $a>1$, on $\mathbb{R}^2$, show
  there is a sequence of rotationally symmetric metrics on $\mathbb{R}^2$ with
  $\sec\le0$, $\inj=\infty$ converging to it in the Gromov--Hausdorff
  topology.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-12
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.12
entries.body:
Show that the class of spaces with $\inj\ge R$ and
  $|\nabla^k\Ric|\le\Lambda$ for $k=0,\dots,m$ is compact in the
  $C^{m+1,\alpha}$ topology.

deps.title: Thm.\ 11.3.6 — Fundamental Theorem of Convergence Theory
deps.label: thm:pet-ch11-fundamental-theorem-convergence

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-13
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.13
entries.body:
(S.-h.\ Zhu) For the class of complete or compact $n$-manifolds with
  conjugate radius $\ge R$, $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$, $\vol B(p,1)\ge v$, use the
  techniques of \cref{lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma} to show
  a lower injectivity-radius bound, hence compactness in $C^{1,\alpha}$.

deps.title: Lemma 11.4.9 (Cheeger, 1967) — volume lower bound controls injectivity radius
deps.label: lem:pet-ch11-cheeger-injectivity-radius-lemma

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-14
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.14
entries.body:
Using the Eguchi--Hanson metrics, show that no compactness result holds in
  general for the class $|\Ric|\le\Lambda$, $\vol B(p,1)\ge v$ alone; either a
  large lower volume bound (as before) or a lower conjugate-radius bound must
  be assumed.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch11-exercise-11-6-15
entries.title: Exercise 11.6.15
entries.body:
The \emph{weak (harmonic) norm} $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{weak}}_{C^{m,\alpha},r}$
  is defined as in \cref{def:pet-ch11-harmonic-norm}, except the charts
  $\varphi_s:B(0,r)\to U_s$ are only required to be immersions, so the
  inverse is only locally defined but still makes sense to be harmonic.
  \begin{enumerate}
  \item Show that if $(M,g)$ has bounded sectional curvature, then for every
    $Q>0$ there is $r>0$ with $\|(M,g)\|^{\mathrm{weak}}_{C^{m,\alpha},r}\le Q$;
    thus the weak norm is a generalized curvature quantity.
  \item Show the class of manifolds with bounded weak norm is precompact in
    the Gromov--Hausdorff topology.
  \item Show $(M,g)$ is flat iff the weak norm vanishes on all scales.
  \end{enumerate}

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-smooth-deformation-retraction
entries.title: Lem. 12.1.1 — deformation retraction along a gradient flow
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a Riemannian manifold and $f:M\to\mathbb{R}$ a proper smooth
  function. If $f$ has no critical values in the closed interval $[a,b]$, then
  $f^{-1}((-\infty,b])$ and $f^{-1}((-\infty,a])$ are diffeomorphic; more
  precisely, there is a deformation retraction of $f^{-1}((-\infty,b])$ onto
  $f^{-1}((-\infty,a])$, so the inclusion
  $f^{-1}((-\infty,a])\hookrightarrow f^{-1}((-\infty,b])$ is a homotopy
  equivalence.

deps.title: Lem. 12.1.1 — deformation retraction along a gradient flow
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-smooth-deformation-retraction

entries.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular
entries.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete, $K\subset M$ compact, and $r(x)=|xK|$. For
  $x\in M$ let $\overrightarrow{xK}\subset T_xM$ denote the set of initial
  unit velocities of unit-speed minimizing segments from $x$ to $K$ (i.e.\ of
  length $r(x)$); when $K=\{q\}$ this is written $\overrightarrow{xq}$. If $r$
  is smooth at $x$ then $\overrightarrow{xK}$ has a single element and
  $-\nabla r(x)=\overrightarrow{xK}$. In general $x$ is \emph{regular}, or
  \emph{noncritical}, for $r$ if $\overrightarrow{xK}$ lies in an open
  hemisphere of the unit sphere of $T_xM$, equivalently there is a unit
  $v\in T_xM$ with $\angle(v,\overrightarrow{xK})<\pi/2$ for every
  $\overrightarrow{xK}\in\overrightarrow{xK}$ (any such $v$ is the center of
  such a hemisphere); otherwise $x$ is a \emph{critical point} of $r$. More
  generally, for $\alpha\in(0,\pi]$, $x$ is \emph{$\alpha$-regular} for $r$ if
  there is a unit $v\in T_xM$ with $\angle(v,\overrightarrow{xK})<\alpha$ for
  every $\overrightarrow{xK}\in\overrightarrow{xK}$ (so regular means
  $\pi/2$-regular), and $R_\alpha(x,K)\subset T_xM$ is the set of all such
  unit directions $v$ at an $\alpha$-regular point $x$.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch12-regular-point-properties
entries.title: Prop. 12.1.2 — properties of regular points
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is complete, $K\subset M$ compact, $r(x)=|xK|$. Then:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item $\overrightarrow{xK}$ is closed, hence compact, for every $x\in M$.
    \item The set of $\alpha$-regular points is open in $M$.
    \item $R_\alpha(x,K)$ is convex for every $\alpha\le\pi/2$.
    \item If $U\subset M$ is open and every point of $U$ is $\alpha$-regular,
      there is a unit vector field $X$ on $U$ with $X|_x\in R_\alpha(x,K)$ for
      all $x\in U$; moreover, if $c$ is an integral curve of $X$ and $s<t$,
      \[
        |c(s)K|-|c(t)K|\ge\cos(\alpha)(t-s).
      \]
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-retraction-lemma
entries.title: Lem. 12.1.3 — Grove--Shiohama retraction lemma
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete and $r(x)=|xK|$ the distance to a compact submanifold
  $K\subset M$. If every point of $r^{-1}([a,b])$ is $\alpha$-regular for
  $\alpha<\pi/2$, then $r^{-1}((-\infty,b])$ deformation retracts onto
  $r^{-1}((-\infty,a])$.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-retraction-remark
entries.title: Rem. 12.1.4 — smooth approximation of the retraction
entries.body:
The original Grove--Shiohama construction shows more: on the region where the
  distance function has no critical points it can be approximated by smooth
  functions without critical points, a fact that has been important elsewhere.

entries.label: cor:pet-ch12-normal-bundle-diffeomorphism
entries.title: Cor. 12.1.5 — diffeomorphism to the normal bundle
entries.body:
Suppose $K$ is a compact submanifold of a complete Riemannian manifold $(M,g)$
  and $|xK|$ is regular everywhere on $M-K$. Then $M$ is diffeomorphic to the
  normal bundle of $K$ in $M$. In particular, if $K=\{p\}$, $M$ is diffeomorphic
  to $\mathbb{R}^n$.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

deps.title: Cor. 12.1.5 — diffeomorphism to the normal bundle
deps.label: cor:pet-ch12-normal-bundle-diffeomorphism

entries.label: def:pet-ch12-hinge
entries.title: hinge
entries.body:
A \emph{hinge} in $(M,g)$ consists of two segments (or geodesics)
  $\overline{px}$ and $\overline{xy}$ that form an interior angle
  $\alpha=\angle(\overrightarrow{xp},\overrightarrow{xy})$ at the common vertex
  $x$, where the specified directions are tangent to the given segments.

entries.label: def:pet-ch12-triangle
entries.title: triangle
entries.body:
A \emph{triangle} in $(M,g)$ consists of three segments (or geodesics)
  $\overline{xy}$, $\overline{yz}$, $\overline{zx}$ meeting pairwise at the three
  vertices $x,y,z$.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-comparison-hinge-triangle-exist
entries.title: Lem. 12.2.1 — existence of comparison hinges/triangles
entries.body:
Suppose $(M,g)$ is complete with $\sec\ge k$. Then for each hinge or triangle
  in $M$ there is a comparison hinge or triangle in the space form $S_k^n$ where
  the corresponding segments have the same lengths and, in the hinge case, the
  angle at the corresponding vertex is the same.

entries.label: prop:pet-ch12-law-of-cosines
entries.title: Prop. 12.2.3 — law of cosines in $S_k^n$
entries.body:
Let a triangle in $S_k^n$ have side lengths $a,b,c$ and let $\alpha$ be the
  angle opposite $a$. Then
  \[
    k=0:\ a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos\alpha;\qquad
    k=-1:\ \cosh a=\cosh b\cosh c-\sinh b\sinh c\cos\alpha;
  \]
  \[
    k=1:\ \cos a=\cos b\cos c+\sin b\sin c\cos\alpha.
  \]

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-hessian-comparison
entries.title: Lem. 12.2.4 — Hessian comparison
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete, $p\in M$, $r(x)=|xp|$. If $\sec M\ge k$, then in the
  support sense everywhere $\mathrm{Hess}_kr\le(1-kt)g$, where $\mathrm{Hess}_k$
  denotes the Hessian of the modified distance function $f_k=f_k\circ r$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-toponogov
entries.title: Thm. 12.2.2 — Toponogov comparison theorem, 1959
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a complete Riemannian manifold with $\sec\ge k$.
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Hinge version.} For any hinge with vertices $p,x,y\in M$ forming
      angle $\alpha$ at $x$, and any comparison hinge in $S_k^n$ with vertices
      $p_k,x_k,y_k$ (\cref{lem:pet-ch12-comparison-hinge-triangle-exist}),
      $|py|\le|p_ky_k|$.
    \item \emph{Triangle version.} For any triangle in $M$, the interior angles
      are no smaller than the corresponding interior angles of a comparison
      triangle in $S_k^n$.
  \end{itemize}

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-toponogov-segment-remark
entries.title: Rem. 12.2.5 — role of segments in the proof
entries.body:
The proof never uses that the objects being compared in $M$ are segments; it
  only requires that the geodesics used in $S_k^n$ are segments. For $k\le0$
  this is automatic. For $k>0$ it means the comparison geodesic must have length
  $<\pi/\sqrt k$, the condition used in the $k=1$ case of the proof.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-quarter-pinched-sphere
entries.title: Thm. 12.3.1 — quarter-pinched sphere theorem, Rauch--Berger--Klingenberg
entries.body:
If $M$ is a simply connected closed Riemannian manifold with
  $1\le\sec\le4-\delta$, then $M$ is homeomorphic to a sphere.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-diameter-sphere
entries.title: Thm. 12.3.2 — Berger 1962 and Grove--Shiohama diameter sphere theorem, 1977
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is closed with $\sec\ge1$ and $\mathrm{diam}>\pi/2$, then $M$ is
  homeomorphic to a sphere.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

deps.title: Thm. 12.3.2 — Berger 1962 and Grove--Shiohama diameter sphere theorem, 1977
deps.label: thm:pet-ch12-diameter-sphere

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-cross-pinching
entries.title: Thm. 12.3.3 — Brendle--Schoen 2008 and Petersen--Tao 2009
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be simply connected of dimension $n$. There is $\varepsilon(n)>0$
  such that if $1\le\sec\le4+\varepsilon$, then $M$ is diffeomorphic to a sphere
  or to one of the compact rank-one symmetric spaces (CROSS) $\mathbb{CP}^{n/2}$,
  $\mathbb{HP}^{n/4}$, $\mathbb{OP}^2$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-diameter-classification
entries.title: Thm. 12.3.4 — Grove--Gromoll 1987 and Wilking 2001

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-gromov-critical-point-estimate
entries.title: Lem. 12.4.2 — Gromov's critical point estimate, 1981
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is a complete open manifold with $\sec\ge0$, then for every $p\in M$
  the distance function $|xp|$ has no critical points outside some ball
  $B(p,R)$. In particular $M$ has the topology of a compact manifold with
  boundary.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: def:pet-ch12-totally-convex
entries.title: totally convex
entries.body:
A subset $A\subset M$ of a Riemannian manifold is \emph{totally convex} if
  every geodesic in $M$ joining two points of $A$ lies entirely in $A$.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch12-totally-convex-cylinder
entries.title: Ex. 12.4.3 — souls of the flat cylinder
entries.body:
On the flat cylinder $\mathbb{R}\times S^1$, every circle $\{p\}\times S^1$ is
  a geodesic and totally convex; no point of $M$ is totally convex, and every
  such circle is a soul.

entries.label: ex:pet-ch12-totally-convex-paraboloid
entries.title: Ex. 12.4.4 — soul of a paraboloid of revolution
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a smooth rotationally symmetric metric
  $dr^2+\rho^2(r)d\theta^2$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ with $\rho'<0$ (a paraboloid of
  revolution). Every geodesic from the origin $r=0$ is a ray to infinity, so the
  origin is a totally convex soul; most other points admit geodesic loops.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-concave-superlevel-convex
entries.title: Lem. 12.4.5 — superlevel sets of concave functions
entries.body:
If $f:(M,g)\to\mathbb{R}$ is concave, meaning $\mathrm{Hess}\,f\le0$ weakly
  everywhere, then every superlevel set $A=\{x\in M\mid f(x)\ge a\}$ is totally
  convex.

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.5 — superlevel sets of concave functions
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-concave-superlevel-convex

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-busemann-proper-concave
entries.title: Lem. 12.4.6 — properness and concavity of the Busemann infimum
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete, noncompact, $\sec\ge0$, and $p\in M$. Let
  $R_p=\{c:[0,\infty)\to M\mid c(0)=p\}$ be the set of rays from $p$, and set
  $f=\inf_{c\in R_p}b_c$, where $b_c$ is the Busemann function of $c$. Then $f$
  is proper and concave.

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.5 — superlevel sets of concave functions
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-concave-superlevel-convex

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.6 — properness and concavity of the Busemann infimum
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-busemann-proper-concave

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-totally-convex-boundary-structure
entries.title: Lem. 12.4.7 — interior, boundary, and the supporting hyperplane property
entries.body:
If $A\subset(M,g)$ is closed and totally convex, then $A$ has an interior
  $\mathrm{int}\,A$ and a boundary $\partial A=A-\mathrm{int}\,A$, where
  $\mathrm{int}\,A$ is a totally convex submanifold of $M$ with closure $A$.
  Moreover for each $x\in\partial A$ there is an inward vector
  $w\in T_xM$ such that every segment $\overline{xy}$ with $y\in\mathrm{int}\,A$
  satisfies $\angle(w,\overline{xy})<\pi/2$ (the \emph{supporting hyperplane
  property}); in particular the distance function to a subset of
  $\mathrm{int}\,A$ has no critical points on $\partial A$.

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.7 — interior, boundary, and the supporting hyperplane property
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-totally-convex-boundary-structure

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-distance-to-boundary-concave
entries.title: Lem. 12.4.8 — concavity and uniqueness of distance to the boundary
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ have $\sec\ge0$ and let $A\subset M$ be totally convex. The
  function $r:A\to\mathbb{R}$, $r(x)=|x\partial A|$, is concave on $A$. When
  $\sec>0$, any maximum of $r$ is unique.

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.8 — concavity and uniqueness of distance to the boundary
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-distance-to-boundary-concave

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.7 — interior, boundary, and the supporting hyperplane property
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-totally-convex-boundary-structure

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem
entries.title: Thm. 12.4.1 — soul theorem, Gromoll--Meyer 1969 and Cheeger--Gromoll 1972
entries.body:
If $(M,g)$ is a complete noncompact Riemannian manifold with $\sec\ge0$, then
  $M$ contains a soul $S\subset M$: a closed totally convex submanifold such
  that $M$ is diffeomorphic to the normal bundle of $S$. When $\sec>0$, the soul
  is a point and $M$ is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$.

deps.title: Thm. 12.4.1 — soul theorem, Gromoll--Meyer 1969 and Cheeger--Gromoll 1972
deps.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.5 — superlevel sets of concave functions
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-concave-superlevel-convex

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.6 — properness and concavity of the Busemann infimum
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-busemann-proper-concave

deps.title: Cor. 12.1.5 — diffeomorphism to the normal bundle
deps.label: cor:pet-ch12-normal-bundle-diffeomorphism

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.8 — concavity and uniqueness of distance to the boundary
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-distance-to-boundary-concave

deps.title: Lem. 12.4.7 — interior, boundary, and the supporting hyperplane property
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-totally-convex-boundary-structure

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-uniqueness-submetry
entries.title: Thm. 12.4.9 — Sharafutdinov 1978 and Perel'man 1993
entries.body:
Let $S$ be a soul of a complete Riemannian manifold with $\sec\ge0$, arising
  from the construction of \cref{thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem}.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item (Sharafutdinov) There is a distance-nonincreasing map
      $\mathrm{Sh}:M\to S$ with $\mathrm{Sh}|_S=\mathrm{id}$; in particular all
      souls of $M$ are isometric to each other.
    \item (Perel'man) $\mathrm{Sh}$ is a submetry; consequently $S$ is a point
      whenever all sectional curvatures based at some single point of $M$ are
      positive.
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Thm. 12.4.1 — soul theorem, Gromoll--Meyer 1969 and Cheeger--Gromoll 1972
deps.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-corank-bound
entries.title: Lem. 12.5.2 — bounded corank
entries.body:
Fix a Riemannian $n$-manifold $M$ with $\sec\ge0$. The \emph{corank} of
  $A\subset M$ is the largest $k$ such that there are balls
  $B(p_1,r_1),\dots,B(p_k,r_k)$ with: (a) a critical point $x_i$ for $p_i$ with
  $|p_ix_i|=10r_i$; (b) $r_i\ge3r_{i-1}$ for $i\ge2$; (c)
  $A\subset\bigcap_iB(p_i,r_i)$. The corank of any $A\subset M$ is bounded by
  $100^n$.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-mayer-vietoris-rank
entries.title: Lem. 12.5.3 — Mayer--Vietoris rank inequality
entries.body:
Let $B_i^j$, $i=1,\dots,m$, $j=0,\dots,n+1$, be bounded open subsets of an
  $n$-manifold $M$ with $\overline{B_i^j}\subset B_i^{j+1}$. Then
  \[
    \mathrm{rank}_*\Big(\bigcup_iB_i^0\subset\bigcup_iB_i^{n+1}\Big)
    \le\sum_{l=0}^{n}\sum_{i_0<\cdots<i_l}
    \mathrm{rank}_*\Big(\bigcap_{s=0}^lB_{i_s}^0\subset\bigcap_{s=0}^lB_{i_s}^{n+1-l}\Big).
  \]

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-content-ratio-bound
entries.title: Lem. 12.5.4 — content ratio bound
entries.body:
Let $M$ be a Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\sec\ge0$, let
  $\mathrm{cont}\,B(p,r)=\mathrm{rank}_*(B(p,r/2)\subset B(p,r))$ be the
  \emph{content} of a ball, and let $\mathcal{C}(k)$ be the largest content of
  any ball of corank $\ge k$ (\cref{lem:pet-ch12-corank-bound}). There is
  $C(n)$, depending only on $n$, with $\mathcal{C}(k)\le
  C(n)\,\mathcal{C}(k+1)$.

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-betti-number-finiteness
entries.title: Thm. 12.5.1 — Gromov's finiteness theorem, 1978 and 1981

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence
entries.title: Lem. 12.6.2 — existence of a uniform regularity angle
entries.body:
For an integer $n>1$ and $v,D,k>0$, there are
  $\alpha=\alpha(n,D,v,k)\in(0,\pi/2)$ and $\delta=\delta(n,D,v,k)>0$ such that
  if $(M,g)$ is a Riemannian $n$-manifold with $\mathrm{diam}\le D$,
  $\mathrm{vol}\ge v$, $\sec\ge-k^2$, and $p,q\in M$ satisfy $|pq|\le\delta$,
  then $p$ is $\alpha$-regular for $q$ or $q$ is $\alpha$-regular for $p$.

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.2 — existence of a uniform regularity angle
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence

entries.label: cor:pet-ch12-diagonal-alpha-regular
entries.title: Cor. 12.6.3 — $\alpha$-regularity of the diagonal
entries.body:
With $\alpha,\delta$ as in \cref{lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence}, in
  $M\times M$ with the product metric let $\Delta=\{(x,x)\mid x\in M\}$. Any
  point within distance $\delta/\sqrt2$ of $\Delta$ is $\alpha$-regular for
  $\Delta$.

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.2 — existence of a uniform regularity angle
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence

entries.label: cor:pet-ch12-close-maps-homotopic
entries.title: Cor. 12.6.4 — nearby maps are homotopic
entries.body:
With $\delta$ as in \cref{lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence}, if
  $f_0,f_1:X\to M$ are continuous with $|f_0(x)f_1(x)|<\delta$ for all $x\in X$,
  then $f_0,f_1$ are homotopic.

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-simplex-extension
entries.title: Lem. 12.6.5 — extending nearby points to a simplex
entries.body:
With $C=\sqrt2/\cos\alpha$ and $\delta$ as above, suppose
  $p_0,\dots,p_k\in B(p,r)\subset M$ and $2r(C^k-1)/(C-1)<\delta$. Then there is
  a continuous map $f:\Delta^k\to B\big(p,r+2rC(C^k-1)/(C-1)\big)$ with
  $f(e_i)=p_i$, natural with respect to restriction to faces.

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.5 — extending nearby points to a simplex
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-simplex-extension

entries.label: lem:pet-ch12-gh-close-homotopy-equivalent
entries.title: Lem. 12.6.6 — homotopy equivalence from Gromov--Hausdorff closeness
entries.body:
There is $\varepsilon=\varepsilon(n,k,v,D)>0$ such that if $(M,g_1)$,
  $(N,g_2)$ are Riemannian $n$-manifolds with $\mathrm{diam}\le D$,
  $\mathrm{vol}\ge v$, $\sec\ge-k^2$, and Gromov--Hausdorff distance
  $d_{GH}(M,N)<\varepsilon$, then $M$ and $N$ are homotopy equivalent.

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.6 — homotopy equivalence from Gromov--Hausdorff closeness
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-gh-close-homotopy-equivalent

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.5 — extending nearby points to a simplex
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-simplex-extension

entries.label: thm:pet-ch12-homotopy-finiteness
entries.title: Thm. — Grove--Petersen homotopy finiteness theorem, 1988
entries.body:
For an integer $n>1$ and $v,D,k>0$, the class of Riemannian $n$-manifolds with
  $\mathrm{diam}\le D$, $\mathrm{vol}\ge v$, $\sec\ge-k^2$ contains only finitely
  many homotopy types.

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.6 — homotopy equivalence from Gromov--Hausdorff closeness
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-gh-close-homotopy-equivalent

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-1
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.1
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be a closed simply connected positively curved manifold. Show
  that if $M$ contains a totally geodesic closed hypersurface, then $M$ is
  homeomorphic to a sphere. (Hint: first show the hypersurface is orientable,
  then that the signed distance function to it has only two critical points, a
  maximum and a minimum; this also shows it suffices to assume
  $H^1(M,\mathbb{Z}_2)=0$.)

deps.title:
segment direction sets; regular, critical, and
  $\alpha$-regular points
deps.label: def:pet-ch12-alpha-regular

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-2
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.2
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete noncompact with $\sec\ge0$ and soul $S\subset M$.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item If $X\in TS$ and $V\in T^\perp S$, show $\sec(X,V)=0$.
    \item If $S$ has codimension $1$ with trivial normal bundle, show
      $(M,g)=(S\times\mathbb{R},g_S+dr^2)$.
    \item If $S$ has codimension $1$ with nontrivial normal bundle, show a
      double cover splits as in (2).
  \end{enumerate}

deps.title: Thm. 12.4.1 — soul theorem, Gromoll--Meyer 1969 and Cheeger--Gromoll 1972
deps.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-3
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.3
entries.body:
Show that the solution of $\ddot\xi=-(1+\varepsilon)\xi$,
  $\xi(0)=\psi(0)>0$, $\dot\xi(0)=\dot\psi(0)>0$ is
  \[
    \xi(t)=\sqrt{\psi(0)^2+\frac{\dot\psi(0)^2}{1+\varepsilon}}\,
    \sin\Big(\sqrt{1+\varepsilon}\,t+\arctan\Big(\frac{\psi(0)\sqrt{1+\varepsilon}}{\dot\psi(0)}\Big)\Big).
  \]

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-4
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.4
entries.body:
Show that the converse of Toponogov's theorem holds: if for some $k$ the
  conclusion of \cref{thm:pet-ch12-toponogov} holds when hinges (or triangles)
  are compared to the same objects in $S_k^2$, then $\sec\ge k$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-5
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.5
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete. Show that if all sectional curvatures on $B(p,2R)$
  are $\ge k$, then Toponogov's comparison theorem
  (\cref{thm:pet-ch12-toponogov}) holds for hinges and triangles in $B(p,R)$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-6
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.6
entries.body:
Let $(M,g)$ be complete with $\sec\ge k$ and $f(x)=|xq|-|xp|$, with $|xq|$,
  $|xp|$ smooth at $x$. Show that \cref{thm:pet-ch12-toponogov} bounds
  $|\nabla f|_x$ from below in terms of the distance from $x$ to a segment from
  $q$ to $p$.

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-7
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.7 — Heintze and Karcher
entries.body:
Let $c\subset(M,g)$ be a geodesic in a Riemannian $n$-manifold with
  $\sec\ge-k^2$, and let $T(c,R)$ be the normal tube of radius $R$ around $c$
  (points joined to $c$ by a perpendicular segment of length $\le R$). In
  adapted tube coordinates $(r,s,\theta)$ the metric satisfies, as $r\to0$,
  \[
    g(r)=\begin{pmatrix}1&&\\&\ddots&\\&&0\end{pmatrix}
    +\begin{pmatrix}0&&\\&\ddots&\\&&1\end{pmatrix}r^2+O(r^3),
  \]
  with the first matrix having a single $0$ in the $s$-direction entry and the
  second a single $0$ there. Use the lower curvature bound to bound the volume
  density on the tube from above, and conclude
  $\mathrm{vol}\,T(c,R)\le f(n,k,R,L(c))$ for a continuous $f$ with
  $f\to0$ as $L(c)\to0$. Use this to prove Lem.\ 11.4.9 of the source and
  \cref{lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence}, showing Toponogov's theorem is
  not needed for the latter.

deps.title: Lem. 12.6.2 — existence of a uniform regularity angle
deps.label: lem:pet-ch12-alpha-regular-existence

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-8
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.8
entries.body:
Show that any vector bundle over $S^2$ admits a complete metric of
  nonnegative sectional curvature. (Hint: $k$-dimensional bundles over $S^2$
  are classified by $\pi_1(SO(k))$; there is one $1$-dimensional bundle, the
  $2$-dimensional bundles are parametrized by $\mathbb{Z}$, and for $k>2$ there
  are two $k$-dimensional bundles.)

deps.title: Thm. 12.4.1 — soul theorem, Gromoll--Meyer 1969 and Cheeger--Gromoll 1972
deps.label: thm:pet-ch12-soul-theorem

entries.label: rem:pet-ch12-ex-12-8-9
entries.title: Exercise 12.8.9
entries.body:
Use Toponogov's theorem (\cref{thm:pet-ch12-toponogov}) to show that the
  Busemann function $b_r$ is convex when $\sec\ge0$.
